blocks:
Ayahs

صٓ ۚ وَٱلْقُرْءَانِ ذِى ٱلذِّكْرِ (١)
Suad. By the Quran bearing the Reminder, (1)
بَلِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فِى عِزَّةٍ وَشِقَاقٍ (٢)
the faithless indeed dwell in conceit and defiance. (2)
كَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن قَرْنٍ فَنَادَوا۟ وَّلَاتَ حِينَ مَنَاصٍ (٣)
How many a generation We have destroyed before them! They cried out [for help], but gone was the time for escape. (3)
وَعَجِبُوٓا۟ أَن جَآءَهُم مُّنذِرٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ هَـٰذَا سَـٰحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ (٤)
They consider it odd that there should come to them a warner from among themselves, and the faithless say, ‘This is a magician, a mendacious liar.’ (4)
أَجَعَلَ ٱلْـَٔالِهَةَ إِلَـٰهًا وَٰحِدًا ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ عُجَابٌ (٥)
‘Has he reduced the gods to one god? This is indeed an odd thing!’ (5)
وَٱنطَلَقَ ٱلْمَلَأُ مِنْهُمْ أَنِ ٱمْشُوا۟ وَٱصْبِرُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ ءَالِهَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَشَىْءٌ يُرَادُ (٦)
Their elite go about [urging others]: ‘Go and stand by your gods! This is indeed the desirable thing [to do]. (6)
مَا سَمِعْنَا بِهَـٰذَا فِى ٱلْمِلَّةِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا ٱخْتِلَـٰقٌ (٧)
We did not hear of this in the latter-day creed. This is nothing but a fabrication. (7)
أَءُنزِلَ عَلَيْهِ ٱلذِّكْرُ مِنۢ بَيْنِنَا ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّن ذِكْرِى ۖ بَل لَّمَّا يَذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابِ (٨)
Has the Reminder been sent down to him out of [all of] us?’ Indeed, they are in doubt concerning My Reminder. Indeed, they have not yet tasted My punishment. (8)
أَمْ عِندَهُمْ خَزَآئِنُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْوَهَّابِ (٩)
Do they possess the treasuries of the mercy of your Lord, the All-mighty, the All-munificent? (9)
أَمْ لَهُم مُّلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ فَلْيَرْتَقُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَسْبَـٰبِ (١٠)
Do they own the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them? [If so,] let them ascend [to the higher spheres] by the means [of ascension]. (10)
جُندٌ مَّا هُنَالِكَ مَهْزُومٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَحْزَابِ (١١)
[They are but] a host routed out there of the factions. (11)
كَذَّبَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَعَادٌ وَفِرْعَوْنُ ذُو ٱلْأَوْتَادِ (١٢)
Before them Noah’s people impugned [their apostle] and [so did the people of] ‘Ad, and Pharaoh, the Impaler [of his victims], (12)
وَثَمُودُ وَقَوْمُ لُوطٍ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ لْـَٔيْكَةِ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْأَحْزَابُ (١٣)
and Thamud, and the people of Lot, and the inhabitants of Aykah: those were the factions. (13)
إِن كُلٌّ إِلَّا كَذَّبَ ٱلرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ عِقَابِ (١٤)
There was not any one but such as impugned the apostles; so My retribution became due [against them]. (14)
وَمَا يَنظُرُ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً وَٰحِدَةً مَّا لَهَا مِن فَوَاقٍ (١٥)
These [too] do not await but a single Cry, which will not grant [them] any respite. (15)
وَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا عَجِّل لَّنَا قِطَّنَا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ (١٦)
They say, ‘Our Lord! Hasten on for us our share before the Day of Reckoning.’ (16)

(Commentary)

The discourse in this Surah revolves around the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) being a warner (منذراً) through the Remembrance (الذكر) — the divine message and revelation — sent down to him from Allah, the Glorified, which calls toward monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the sincerity of servitude (إخلاص العبودية) to Him, the Exalted.

It begins by mentioning the arrogance (اعتزاز) of the disbelievers and their schism (شقاقهم) — in short, their haughtiness (استكبارهم) in refusing to follow him, their lack of faith in him, their barring of people from his path, and their uttering of falsehood in that regard, which is refuted in one section.

Then, it commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to be patient and mentions the stories of His former servants in another section. Subsequently, it describes the ultimate fate of the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness and protect themselves against divine punishment by fulfilling duties and avoiding sins — and the transgressors (الطاغين) in a separate section. Finally, it commands the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to convey his warning and his call to the monotheism of Allah, explaining that the destination of the followers of Satan is the Fire, according to what Allah decreed on the day He commanded the angels to prostrate to Adam; Iblis refused, so He stoned him and decreed the Fire for him and those who follow him, all in one section.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context (سياق) of its verses.

His saying, the Exalted: ص وَالْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي عِزَّةٍ وَشِقاقٍ — Sad. By the Quran containing the Remembrance. Rather the faithless are in [the grip of] pride and defiance. (38:1-2). What is meant by the Remembrance (الذكر) is the remembrance of Allah, the Exalted, through His monotheism and the true teachings (المعارف الحقة) that branch out from it, such as the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and the life hereafter — Prophethood (النبوة), and others. Arrogance (العزة) refers to refusal and disdain, and schism (الشقاق) refers to opposition. It is stated in Majma‘ al-Bayan: "Its root is for each of the two parties to be in a shiqq, meaning a side; from this, it is said: 'So-and-so split the staff' (شق فلان العصا) when he opposes." End quote.

What is understood from the context of the verses is that His saying: وَالْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ is an oath (قسم), similar to what is found in His sayings: يس وَالْقُرْآنِ الْحَكِيمِ (Ya Sin. By the wise Quran), ق وَالْقُرْآنِ الْمَجِيدِ (Qaf. By the glorious Quran), and ن وَالْقَلَمِ (Nun. By the Pen). It is not a conjunction (عطف) to what preceded it. As for the subject of the oath (المقسم عليه), that which is indicated by the digression (الإضراب) in His saying: بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي عِزَّةٍ وَشِقاقٍ is that it is a matter which the people refuse to accept and disbelieve in out of arrogance and defiance, and many generations have perished because of it. Then He mentions the warning of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), what the disbelievers said against him, and what their elite (ملؤهم) commanded regarding his warning (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). This indicates that the subject of the oath is something like our saying: "Indeed, you are among the warners." This is also supported by the repeated mention in the Surah of his warning (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) through the Remembrance.

Many views have been expressed regarding the grammatical position (الإعراب) and meaning of His saying: ص وَالْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ, most of which yield no real result; we have omitted mentioning them due to their lack of benefit.

The meaning — and Allah knows best — is: I swear by the Quran that contains the Remembrance — indeed, you are among the warners — rather, those who disbelieve are in a state of refusal to accept and follow it, and in opposition to it.

His saying, the Exalted: كَمْ أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنْ قَرْنٍ فَنادَوْا وَلاتَ حِينَ مَناصٍ — How many a generation We have destroyed before them! They cried out [for help], but it was not the time for escape. (38:3). A generation (القرن) refers to the people of a single era. Escape (المناص), with the letter nun, is a verbal noun from nasa yanusu, meaning to delay or fall back, just as with the letter ba it means to advance, according to what is in al-Majma‘. It is also said to mean fleeing (الفرار).

The meaning is: Frequently have We destroyed before these disbelievers a generation and a nation (أمة) because of their denial of the warning messengers. So they cried out when the punishment descended, wailing as in their saying: "O woe to us! Indeed, we were wrongdoers," or seeking help from Allah, the Glorified, but it was not the time for the delay of seizure and punishment, or it was not the time for flight.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعَجِبُوا أَنْ جاءَهُمْ مُنْذِرٌ مِنْهُمْ وَقالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا ساحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ — They consider it odd that a warner should have come to them from among themselves, and the faithless say, ‘This is a magician, a liar. (38:4). That is, they marveled at the coming of a warner from their own kind, being a human being, for idolatry (الوثنية) denies the messengership of humans.

His saying: وَقالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا ساحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ refers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), accusing him of sorcery (السحر) because they were unable to produce the like of what he brought, which is the Quran, and of lying (الكذب) because of their claim that he fabricates against Allah by attributing the Quran and the true knowledge within it to Him, the Exalted.

His saying, the Exalted: أَجَعَلَ الْآلِهَةَ إِلهاً واحِداً إِنَّ هذا لَشَيْءٌ عُجابٌ — ‘Has he made the gods into one God? This is indeed a wonderful thing!’ (38:5). ‘Ujab (عجاب), with a light jim, is an intensive form (اسم مبالغة) of wonder (العجب), and with a doubled jim (‘ujjab), it is even more intensive.

This is a continuation of the speech of the disbelievers, and the interrogative (الاستفهام) is for expressing amazement. "Making" (الجعل) here means "rendering" (التصيير), and as has been said, it is a rendering in terms of speech, belief, and claim, not in terms of reality, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَجَعَلُوا الْمَلائِكَةَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ عِبادُ الرَّحْمنِ إِناثاً (And they designate the angels—who are servants of the All-beneficent—as females) [Az-Zukhruf: 19]. Thus, the meaning of his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) making the gods into one God is his nullification of the divinity of gods besides Allah and his ruling that the [true] God is Allah, there is no god but He.

His saying, the Exalted: وَانْطَلَقَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْهُمْ أَنِ امْشُوا وَاصْبِرُوا عَلى آلِهَتِكُمْ إِنَّ هذا لَشَيْءٌ يُرادُ — Their elite go about [saying], ‘Go and stand by your gods! This is indeed a thing intended [against you]. (38:6). Attributing the "going forth" (الانطلاق) to their elite and nobles, and their saying what they said, suggests that the nobles of the Quraysh gathered before the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to solve the problem of his call to monotheism and his rejection of the gods through a kind of conciliation. They spoke to him regarding this, but he did not agree with them on any of it. Then they went forth and said to one another, or said to their followers: "Go and be patient..." etc. This supports what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول), which will come in the following traditional discussion, if Allah wills.

His saying: أَنِ امْشُوا وَاصْبِرُوا عَلى آلِهَتِكُمْ implies the word "saying" (قائلين), i.e., saying: "Go and be patient with your gods and do not abandon their worship, even if he disparages and criticizes them." The apparent context is that this was said by some of them to others, though it is possible it was said to their followers.

His saying: إِنَّ هذا لَشَيْءٌ يُرادُ apparently refers to what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) calls for and seeks, and that his objective is something naturally desired, namely sovereignty and leadership, and that he only made the call a pretext for it. This is similar to the saying of the elite of the people of Noah to their commoners: ما هذا إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُرِيدُ أَنْ يَتَفَضَّلَ عَلَيْكُمْ (This is only a human like you, who seeks to have an advantage over you) [Al-Mu’minun: 24].

It has also been said the meaning is: "Indeed, this persistence we have witnessed from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in what he seeks and his firmness in his religion is a great thing intended by him."

It has also been said the meaning is: "Indeed, this matter is one of the calamities of time intended against us, so there is no recourse but to go and be patient."

It has also been said the meaning is: "Indeed, patience is a praised character trait intended of us in such situations." Other views have been mentioned, but they are weak and do not suit the context.

His saying, the Exalted: ما سَمِعْنا بِهذا فِي الْمِلَّةِ الْآخِرَةِ إِنْ هذا إِلَّا اخْتِلاقٌ — ‘We did not hear of this in the latter-day creed. This is nothing but a fabrication. (38:7). By "the latter-day creed" (الملة الآخرة), they meant the religion practiced by the later nations contemporary to them or close to their era, as opposed to the first creeds practiced by the ancients. It is as if they were saying: "This is not from the latter-day creed that the people of the world approve of today, but rather from the fables of the ancients."

It has been said that "the latter-day creed" refers to Christianity because it was the last of the creeds, and they do not profess monotheism but rather the Trinity. Its weakness is obvious, as Christianity did not have the same standing among them as Islam.

His saying: إِنْ هذا إِلَّا اخْتِلاقٌ means it is nothing but a lie and a fabrication (افتعال).

His saying, the Exalted: أَأُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ مِنْ بَيْنِنا — ‘Has the Remembrance been sent down to him out of [all of] us?’ (38:8). This is a rhetorical question (استفهام إنكاري) for the purpose of denial, meaning: "Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) has no merit by which he is preferred over us such that the Remembrance should be sent down to him instead of us." Thus, in denying the exclusivity of the descent of the Remembrance, it is similar to their saying: "You are but a human like us" in denying the exclusivity of messengership.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْ ذِكْرِي بَلْ لَمَّا يَذُوقُوا عَذابِ — ‘Rather they are in doubt about My Remembrance. Rather they have not yet tasted My punishment.’ (38:8). This is a digression (إضراب) from all that they said, meaning: they did not speak out of faith or conviction, but rather they are in doubt regarding My Remembrance, which is the Quran.

Their doubt in it is not due to the obscurity of the proof of prophethood or its inadequacy in providing certainty (اليقين), but rather the attachment of their hearts to the falsehood they possess and their adherence to blind following (التقليد) diverts them from reflecting on the proof of the miraculous divine sign. Thus, they doubted the Remembrance while it is a miraculous sign.

His saying: بَلْ لَمَّا يَذُوقُوا عَذابِ is a further digression, meaning: their denial and lack of faith are not due to their doubt in it, but because, in their rebellion and arrogance, they do not acknowledge its truth even if there were no doubt, until they taste My punishment and are forced to acknowledge it, as others did.

In His saying: لَمَّا يَذُوقُوا عَذابِ, meaning they have not yet tasted My punishment, there is a threat of an impending punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ عِنْدَهُمْ خَزائِنُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ الْعَزِيزِ الْوَهَّابِ — Do they possess the treasuries of the mercy of your Lord, the All-mighty, the All-munificent? (38:9). The speech is in the position of a digression, and "or" (am) is disjunctive (منقطعة). The speech looks back to their saying: "Has the Remembrance been sent down to him out of [all of] us?" meaning: "Rather, do they possess the treasuries of the mercy of your Lord, from which He spends on whom He wills, such that they could withhold it from you? Rather, it belongs to Him, the Exalted, and He knows best where to place His message and whom to distinguish with His mercy."

The conclusion of the speech with His saying: الْعَزِيزِ الْوَهَّابِ is to support the gist of the sentence, meaning: they do not possess any of the treasuries of His mercy because He is the All-mighty (العزيز) — the Invincible whose side cannot be overcome — and no one interferes in His affair; nor can they divert His mercy from anyone because He is the All-munificent (الوهاب) — the Giver of many gifts.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ لَهُمْ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُما فَلْيَرْتَقُوا فِي الْأَسْبابِ — Or does the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them belong to them? Then let them ascend the tracks [to the heaven]! (38:10). "Or" (am) is disjunctive, and the command in His saying: فَلْيَرْتَقُوا is for demonstrating incapacity (التعجيز). Ascending (الارتقاء) means climbing, and the tracks (الأسباب) are the means of ascent (المعارج) and paths (المناهج) by which one seeks to climb to the heavens. It is also possible that "ascending the tracks" refers to employing causes and stratagems by which they might achieve prevention and diversion.

The meaning is: "Rather, do they possess the kingdom of the heavens and the earth such that they have the right to dispose of them and prevent the descent of heavenly revelation to a human on earth? If that is so, then let them climb the ascents of the heavens or let them employ the means and prevent the revelation from descending upon you."

His saying, the Exalted: جُنْدٌ ما هُنالِكَ مَهْزُومٌ مِنَ الْأَحْزابِ — [They are] a small army of the factions, defeated there. (38:11). Defeat (الهزيمة) is abandonment and failure. "Of the factions" (مِنَ الْأَحْزابِ) is an explanation for His saying: "a small army" (جُنْدٌ ما), and the "what" (ma) is for expressing smallness and insignificance (التحقير). The speech is intended to belittle their affair, despite what the outward appearance of their words suggests of self-exaltation and self-admiration.

This is indicated by the indefiniteness of "army" (جُنْدٌ), its supplementation with the word "what" (ma), the reference to their position with "there" (هنالك) which indicates distance, and counting them among the factions (الأحزاب) who allied against the messengers, whose predecessors Allah eradicated, as will be mentioned. Therefore, this army was considered defeated even before their actual defeat.

The meaning is: they are a small, lowly, defeated army there, from among those factions who allied against the messengers and denied them, so My punishment became due upon them.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذَّابَتْ قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ وَعادٌ وَفِرْعَوْنُ ذُو الْأَوْتادِ — The people of Noah denied before them, and [so did] ‘Ad and Pharaoh, the impaler, (38:12) until His saying — فَحَقَّ عِقابِ — so My punishment became due. (38:14). "The impaler" (ذو الأوتاد) is a description of Pharaoh, and awtad is the plural of watad (stake/peg), which is well-known. It is said he was called "the impaler" because he had playgrounds made of stakes upon which people played for him. It is also said because he used to punish those criminals he was angry with using stakes, pinning their hands, feet, and heads to the ground to torture them. It is also said it means "possessor of armies," who are the stakes of the kingdom. Other views have been mentioned, but there is no evidence for any of them to be relied upon.

The "dwellers of the Thicket" (أصحاب الأيكة) are the people of Shu‘ayb, as previously mentioned in Surah al-Hijr and ash-Shu‘ara. His saying: فَحَقَّ عِقابِ means My punishment was realized and established against them, so I destroyed them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما يَنْظُرُ هؤُلاءِ إِلَّا صَيْحَةً واحِدَةً ما لَها مِنْ فَواقٍ — These only await a single Cry, which will have no delay. (38:15). "Looking" (النظر) here means waiting (الانتظار), and fawaq (فواق) means return or a brief respite. The meaning is: these deniers from your nation await nothing but a single Cry that will finish them and destroy them, having no return or respite; it is the punishment of eradication (عذاب الاستئصال).

They said: what is meant by the Cry is the Cry of the Day of Resurrection, because the punishment for the nation of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is delayed until the standing of the Hour. However, you have already learned in the commentary of Surah Yunus that the apparent meaning of the verses of the Book gives the opposite of that; so refer back to it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا رَبَّنا عَجِّلْ لَنا قِطَّنا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ الْحِسابِ — They say, ‘Our Lord! Hasten to us our share before the Day of Reckoning.’ (38:16). The qitt (القط) is the portion or lot. This word is an expression of their seeking to hasten the punishment before the Day of Resurrection, mocking the talk of the Day of Reckoning and the threat of punishment therein.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narrators from Jabir, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him), who said: "Abu Jahl ibn Hisham approached with a group from the Quraysh, and they entered upon Abu Talib and said: 'Indeed, your nephew has harmed us and harmed our gods, so call him and command him to desist from our gods, and we will desist from his God.'"

He said: "So Abu Talib sent for the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and called him. When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) entered, he saw none in the house but polytheists. He said: السلام على من اتبع الهدى — 'Peace be upon him who follows the guidance,' then he sat down. Abu Talib informed him of what they had come for. He said: 'Would they be interested in a word that is better for them than this, by which they would rule the Arabs and tread upon their necks?'"

"Abu Jahl said: 'Yes, and what is this word?' He said: 'You say: لا إله إلا الله — There is no god but Allah.'"

He said: "So they put their fingers in their ears and went out saying: 'We did not hear of this in the latter-day creed; this is nothing but a fabrication.' So Allah sent down regarding their saying: ص وَالْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ until His saying إِلَّا اخْتِلاقٌ."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَعَجِبُوا أَنْ جاءَهُمْ مُنْذِرٌ مِنْهُمْ, he said: "When the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) made the call public, the Quraysh gathered before Abu Talib and said: 'O Abu Talib, your nephew has belittled our intellects, reviled our gods, corrupted our youth, and divided our community. If what drives him to this is poverty, we will collect wealth for him until he is the richest man in Quraysh, and we will make him our king.'"

"Abu Talib informed the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of that. He said: والله لو وضعوا الشمس في يميني والقمر في يساري ما أردته ولكن يعطونني كلمة يملكون بها العرب ويدين لهم بها العجم ويكونون ملوكا في الجنة — 'By Allah, if they were to place the sun in my right hand and the moon in my left, I would not want it. But let them give me one word by which they will rule the Arabs, the non-Arabs will submit to them, and they will be kings in Paradise.' Abu Talib told them that, and they said: 'Yes, and ten words!' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to them: 'You bear witness that there is no god but Allah and that I am the Messenger of Allah.' They said: 'Shall we leave three hundred and sixty gods and worship one God?'"

"So Allah, the Glorified, sent down: وَعَجِبُوا أَنْ جاءَهُمْ مُنْذِرٌ مِنْهُمْ وَقالَ الْكافِرُونَ هذا ساحِرٌ كَذَّابٌ until His saying إِلَّا اخْتِلاقٌ, meaning a muddle. أَأُنْزِلَ عَلَيْهِ الذِّكْرُ مِنْ بَيْنِنا بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْ ذِكْرِي until His saying مِنَ الْأَحْزابِ, meaning those who allied against him on the Day of the Trench (الأحزاب)."

I say: The story is also narrated through Sunni channels. In some of their narrations, when he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) presented the word of monotheism to them, they said to him: "Ask us for something other than this." He said: لو جئتموني بالشمس حتى تضعوها في يدي ما سألتكم غيرها — "If you were to bring me the sun and place it in my hand, I would not ask you for anything else." So they became angry and said... The "word" is a metaphor for their giving him control over the system of the earthly world, for the sun and the moon are among the greatest influences upon it. It seems that the amount of beauty was taken into account for the intended metaphor to be valid.

In al-‘Ilal, with its chain of narrators to Ishaq ibn ‘Ammar, who said: "I asked Abu al-Hasan Musa ibn Ja‘far (peace be upon him): 'How did the prayer become one unit (ركعة) and two prostrations (سجدتين)? And why, when it became two prostrations, did it not become two units?' He said: 'When you ask about something, empty your heart so that you may understand. Indeed, the first prayer the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) prayed, he only prayed it in the heavens before Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, in front of His Throne.'"

"And that was when he was taken on the Night Journey (أسري به) and reached His Throne. He said: 'O Muhammad, draw near to Sad (ص), wash your places of prostration and purify them, and pray to your Lord.' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) drew near to where Allah, Blessed and Exalted be He, commanded him, and he performed ablution (توضأ) and perfected his ablution."

"I said: 'May I be your ransom, what is Sad from which he was commanded to wash?' He said: 'A spring that gushes forth from a pillar among the pillars of the Throne; it is called the Water of Life (ماء الحيوان), and it is what Allah, Mighty and Majestic be He, said: ص وَالْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ...'" the tradition continues.

I say: This meaning — namely, that Sad (ص) is a river that comes out from the leg of the Throne — is narrated in al-Ma‘ani from Sufyan al-Thawri from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him). It is also narrated in Majma‘ al-Bayan from Ibn ‘Abbas that it is one of the names of Allah, the Exalted; he said: "And that is narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him)."

In al-Ma‘ani, with its chain of narrators to al-Asbagh from ‘Ali (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic be He: وَقالُوا رَبَّنا عَجِّلْ لَنا قِطَّنا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ الْحِسابِ, he said: "Their portion of the punishment."

Ayahs

ٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَقُولُونَ وَٱذْكُرْ عَبْدَنَا دَاوُۥدَ ذَا ٱلْأَيْدِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ (١٧)
Be patient over what they say, and remember Our servant, David, [the man] of strength. Indeed he was a penitent [soul]. (17)
إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا ٱلْجِبَالَ مَعَهُۥ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِشْرَاقِ (١٨)
We disposed the mountains to glorify [Allah] with him at evening and dawn, (18)
وَٱلطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً ۖ كُلٌّ لَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ (١٩)
and the birds [as well], mustered [in flocks]; all echoing him [in a chorus]. (19)
وَشَدَدْنَا مُلْكَهُۥ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَفَصْلَ ٱلْخِطَابِ (٢٠)
We consolidated his kingdom and gave him wisdom and conclusive speech. (20)
۞ وَهَلْ أَتَىٰكَ نَبَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَصْمِ إِذْ تَسَوَّرُوا۟ ٱلْمِحْرَابَ (٢١)
Has there not come to you the account of the contenders, when they scaled the wall into the sanctuary? (21)
إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَىٰ دَاوُۥدَ فَفَزِعَ مِنْهُمْ ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ ۖ خَصْمَانِ بَغَىٰ بَعْضُنَا عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا تُشْطِطْ وَٱهْدِنَآ إِلَىٰ سَوَآءِ ٱلصِّرَٰطِ (٢٢)
When they entered into the presence of David, he was alarmed by them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid. [We are only] two contenders: one of us has bullied the other. So judge justly between us, and do not exceed [the bounds of justice], and show us the right path.’ (22)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَآ أَخِى لَهُۥ تِسْعٌ وَتِسْعُونَ نَعْجَةً وَلِىَ نَعْجَةٌ وَٰحِدَةٌ فَقَالَ أَكْفِلْنِيهَا وَعَزَّنِى فِى ٱلْخِطَابِ (٢٣)
‘This brother of mine has ninety-nine ewes, while I have only a single ewe, and [yet] he says, ‘Commit it to my care,’ and he browbeats me in speech.’ (23)
قَالَ لَقَدْ ظَلَمَكَ بِسُؤَالِ نَعْجَتِكَ إِلَىٰ نِعَاجِهِۦ ۖ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلْخُلَطَآءِ لَيَبْغِى بَعْضُهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَقَلِيلٌ مَّا هُمْ ۗ وَظَنَّ دَاوُۥدُ أَنَّمَا فَتَنَّـٰهُ فَٱسْتَغْفَرَ رَبَّهُۥ وَخَرَّ رَاكِعًا وَأَنَابَ ۩ (٢٤)
He said, ‘He has certainly wronged you by asking your ewe in addition to his own ewes, and indeed many partners bully one another, except such as have faith and do righteous deeds, and few are they.’ Then David knew that We had tested him, whereat he pleaded with his Lord for forgiveness, and fell down in prostration and repented. (24)
فَغَفَرْنَا لَهُۥ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَإِنَّ لَهُۥ عِندَنَا لَزُلْفَىٰ وَحُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ (٢٥)
So We forgave him that, and indeed he has [a station of] nearness with Us and a good destination. (25)
يَـٰدَاوُۥدُ إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰكَ خَلِيفَةً فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱحْكُم بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعِ ٱلْهَوَىٰ فَيُضِلَّكَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌۢ بِمَا نَسُوا۟ يَوْمَ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٢٦)
‘O David! Indeed, We have made you a vicegerent on the earth. So judge between people with justice, and do not follow your desires, or they will lead you astray from the way of Allah. Indeed there is a severe punishment for those who stray from the way of Allah, because of their forgetting the Day of Reckoning.’ (26)
وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا بَـٰطِلًا ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ظَنُّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ (٢٧)
We did not create the sky and the earth and whatever is between them in vain. That is a conjecture of the faithless. So woe to the faithless for the Fire! (27)
أَمْ نَجْعَلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ كَٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ كَٱلْفُجَّارِ (٢٨)
Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the Godwary like the vicious? (28)
كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبَـٰرَكٌ لِّيَدَّبَّرُوٓا۟ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ وَلِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ (٢٩)
[This is] a blessed Book that We have sent down to you, so that they may contemplate its signs, and that those who possess intellect may take admonition. (29)

(Commentary)

When the Glorified One recounted how the polytheists accused the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and his truthful call of being a fabrication (اختلاق) and a mere pretext for advancement and leadership, and claimed that he possessed no merit over them to be singled out for messengership (الرسالة) and warning (الإنذار); and then recounted their mockery of the Day of Reckoning and the punishment with which they were being warned—He commanded the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to be patient, that their lapses should not shake him nor weaken his resolve, and that he should remember a number of His servants who were penitent (الأوابين) — those who constantly turn back to Allah in every event that befalls them.

These are nine of the noble prophets mentioned by the Glorified One: David, Solomon, Job, Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Ishmael, Elisha, and Dhul-Kifl (عليه‌السلام). He began with David (عليه‌السلام) and mentioned some of his accounts.

His saying, the Exalted: اصْبِرْ عَلى ما يَقُولُونَ وَاذْكُرْ عَبْدَنا داوُدَ ذَا الْأَيْدِ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ — "Be patient over what they say, and remember Our servant David, [the man] of strength. Indeed he was penitent." (38:17). The term "strength" (الأيد) refers to power (القوة). He (عليه‌السلام) possessed strength in his glorification (التسبيح) of the Exalted, for he would glorify Him and the mountains and birds would glorify along with him; he possessed strength in his dominion, strength in his knowledge, and strength and prowess in wars, having killed the king Goliath (جالوت) as Allah recounted in Surah al-Baqarah.

The term "penitent" (الأواب) is an intensive form derived from "returning" (الأوب), meaning one who returns; what is meant is his frequent returning to his Lord.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا الْجِبالَ مَعَهُ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِشْراقِ — "Indeed We disposed the mountains to glorify with him at evening and sunrise," (38:18). It appears that "with him" (مَعَهُ) is connected to His saying "they glorify" (يُسَبِّحْنَ), and the phrase "with him they glorify" is an explanation of the meaning of "disposed" (التسخير) — the divine act of making something subservient to a specific purpose. The adverbial phrase was placed first because the focus is on their following David and emulating him in glorification. However, His saying, the Exalted, in another place: وَسَخَّرْنا مَعَ داوُدَ الْجِبالَ يُسَبِّحْنَ وَالطَّيْرَ — "And We disposed the mountains to glorify with David, and the birds [as well]" (21:79), supports the connection of the adverbial phrase to "We disposed" (سخرنا). It also occurs in another place in His speech: يا جِبالُ أَوِّبِي مَعَهُ وَالطَّيْرَ — "O mountains, echo [His praise] with him, and [you too] O birds!" (34:10). The terms "evening" (العشي) and "sunrise" (الإشراق) refer to the late afternoon and the morning.

His saying: إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا (Indeed We disposed...) — the particle "Indeed" (إن) here denotes causation (التعليل). This verse and those coordinated with it serve as an explanation of his (عليه‌السلام) being "possessor of strength" in his glorification, dominion, and knowledge, and his being "penitent" toward his Lord.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً كُلٌّ لَهُ أَوَّابٌ — "and the birds as well, mustered: each was penitent to Him." (38:19). The term "mustered" (المحشورة) is from "mustering" (الحشر) — the act of gathering together by force or summons; meaning: We disposed the birds with him, gathered for him, glorifying along with him.

His saying: كُلٌّ لَهُ أَوَّابٌ (each was penitent to Him) is a new sentence (استئناف) confirming what preceded regarding the glorification of the mountains and birds; i.e., each of the mountains and birds is "penitent" (أواب), meaning frequently returning to Us through glorification, for glorification is one of the instances of returning to the Exalted. It is also possible that the pronoun in "to him" (لَهُ) refers to David, though this is less likely.

The support given to David (عليه‌السلام) did not lie in the fundamental fact that Allah made the mountains and birds glorify Him, for everything glorifies the Glorified One; He said: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ وَلكِنْ لا تَفْقَهُونَ تَسْبِيحَهُمْ — "There is not a thing but celebrates His praise, but you do not understand their glorification." (17:44). Rather, the support lay in their glorification coinciding with his glorification and their glorification reaching the ears of people. We have previously discussed the meaning of the glorification of things for the Glorified One in the commentary on the verse: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ, noting that it is with the "tongue of speech" (لسان القال) rather than merely the "tongue of state" (لسان الحال).

His saying, the Exalted: وَشَدَدْنا مُلْكَهُ وَآتَيْناهُ الْحِكْمَةَ وَفَصْلَ الْخِطابِ — "We strengthened his kingdom and gave him wisdom and a decisive judgment." (38:20). Al-Raghib said: "Strengthening" (الشد) is a firm binding; it is said, 'I strengthened the thing' when I made its knot firm. (End quote). Thus, "strengthening the kingdom" is a metaphorical expression (استعارة بالكناية) meaning the fortification of the kingdom and the solidifying of its foundations through awe, armies, treasuries, sound management, and all other means by which a kingdom is empowered.

"Wisdom" (الحكمة) — the firm and precise knowledge that benefits a human being and perfects them — is fundamentally the structure of a type of judgment. It has been said to mean prophethood, or the Psalms (الزبور) and the knowledge of laws, or other things; these are weak views.

"Decisive judgment" (فصل الخطاب) — the ability to distinguish truth from falsehood in speech and to resolve disputes — refers to the disentangling of speech that occurs when one person addresses another, and the distinguishing of its truth from its falsehood. This applies to judging between litigants in their disputes.

It has also been said to mean speech that is purposeful, neither deficient in its brevity nor tedious in its prolixity. Others said "decisive judgment" refers to the phrase "Now then" (أما بعد), and that he (عليه‌السلام) was the first to say it. The following verse: وَهَلْ أَتاكَ نَبَأُ الْخَصْمِ (And has the news of the litigants reached you...) supports what we have presented.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهَلْ أَتاكَ نَبَأُ الْخَصْمِ إِذْ تَسَوَّرُوا الْمِحْرابَ — "Has the news of the litigants reached you, when they climbed the wall into the sanctuary?" (38:21). "Litigants" (الخصم) is a verbal noun like "litigation" (الخصومة), used here to refer to the group in whom the dispute resided. "Climbing the wall" (التسور) means ascending to the top of the wall (السور), which is a high barrier, similar to "ascending the hump" (التسنم) of a camel or "reaching the peak" (التذري) of a mountain. The "sanctuary" (المحراب) has been interpreted as a chamber or an upper room. The interrogative form is for expressing wonder and inciting interest in hearing the news.

The meaning is: Has the news of the disputing people reached you, O Muhammad, when they climbed the wall of the sanctuary—the sanctuary of David (عليه‌السلام)?

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلى داوُدَ فَفَزِعَ مِنْهُمْ — "When they entered David’s presence, and he was panicked by them..." (38:22) to the end of the verse. This "when" (إِذْ) is an adverb for His saying "they climbed" (تَسَوَّرُوا), just as the first "when" was an adverb for "the news of the litigants" (نَبَأُ الْخَصْمِ). The sum of the meaning is that they entered upon David while he was in his sanctuary, not through the usual path, but by climbing the wall and entering from there; hence he was panicked by them when he saw them entering through an unusual way and without permission.

Regarding His saying: فَفَزِعَ مِنْهُمْ (and he was panicked by them), Al-Raghib said: "Panic" (الفزع) is a contraction and aversion that afflicts a human being from a frightening thing; it is of the same genus as "distress" (الجزع). One does not say, 'I was panicked (فزعت) by Allah,' as one says, 'I feared (خفت) Him.' (End quote).

It has been previously mentioned that "dread" (الخشية) is an effect on the heart that entails agitation and anxiety, and it is a blameworthy vice except when it is dread of Allah the Glorified; for this reason, the prophets (عليه‌السلام) did not dread anyone but Him. He said: وَلا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَداً إِلَّا اللهَ — "and who do not fear anyone except Allah." (33:39).

Furthermore, "fear" (الخوف) is the effect of something disliked at the level of action, by preparing that which guards against evil and repels the disliked thing, not at the level of perception. Thus, it is not a blameworthy vice in itself; rather, it is good when it is proper to take precaution. The Exalted said, addressing His Messenger: وَإِمَّا تَخافَنَّ مِنْ قَوْمٍ خِيانَةً — "And if you fear treachery from a people..." (8:58).

Since "panic" (الفزع) is the contraction and aversion resulting from a frightening thing, it is a matter pertaining to the level of action rather than perception. Thus, it was not a vice in itself; rather, it was a virtue upon the realization of something disliked from which one should take precaution. Therefore, there is no harm in attributing it to David (عليه‌السلام) in His saying: فَفَزِعَ مِنْهُمْ, and he is among the prophets who dread none but Allah.

His saying: قالُوا لا تَخَفْ خَصْمانِ بَغى بَعْضُنا عَلى بَعْضٍ — "They said, ‘Do not be afraid. [We are] two litigants, one of whom has wronged the other.’" (38:22). When they saw David’s (عليه‌السلام) panic, they intended to comfort him and calm his fear, so they said: "Do not be afraid" (لا تَخَفْ), which is a prohibition of panic by prohibiting its cause, which is fear. "[We are] two litigants, one of whom has wronged..." (خَصْمانِ بَغى) etc., meaning: We are two litigants—two disputing parties—one of whom has unjustly transgressed against the other.

His saying: فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَنا بِالْحَقِّ وَلا تُشْطِطْ — "‘So judge between us with truth, and do not be unjust...’" (38:22). "Injustice" (الشطط) is transgression (الجور); meaning: Judge between us with a judgment accompanied by truth, do not transgress in your judgment, and guide us to the middle of justice on the path.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هذا أَخِي — "‘Indeed this is my brother...’" (38:23) to the end of the verse, is an explanation of their dispute. His saying: "Indeed this is my brother" is the speech of one individual from one of the two parties, pointing to another from the other party, saying that this is his brother, etc.

By this, the error of those who used this verse to argue that the minimum for a plural (الجمع) is two becomes apparent—based on the appearance of His saying "they climbed" (تَسَوَّرُوا) and "they entered" (دَخَلُوا) as being plural, while His saying "two litigants" (خَصْمانِ) and "this is my brother" (هذا أَخِي) indicates duality.

This is because it is permissible for each side of the duality to contain more than one individual; the Exalted said: هذانِ خَصْمانِ اخْتَصَمُوا فِي رَبِّهِمْ فَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا — "These two are litigants who dispute concerning their Lord. As for those who disbelieve..." (22:19). It is also possible that the original dispute was between two individuals, and then others joined each of them to assist in their claim.

His saying: لَهُ تِسْعٌ وَتِسْعُونَ نَعْجَةً وَلِيَ نَعْجَةٌ واحِدَةٌ فَقالَ أَكْفِلْنِيها وَعَزَّنِي فِي الْخِطابِ — "‘He has ninety-nine ewes, and I have a single ewe. Then he said, ‘‘Entrust it to me,’’ and he overpowered me in speech.’" (38:23). A "ewe" (النعجة) is a female sheep. "Entrust it to me" (أَكْفِلْنِيها) means: Place it under my guardianship and authority. "He overpowered me in speech" (عَزَّنِي فِي الْخِطابِ) means: He defeated me in it. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ لَقَدْ ظَلَمَكَ بِسُؤالِ نَعْجَتِكَ إِلى نِعاجِهِ — "He said, ‘He has certainly wronged you by asking for your ewe [to add it] to his ewes...’" (38:24) until His saying: وَقَلِيلٌ ما هُمْ — "and they are few." This is David’s (عليه‌السلام) response. Perhaps it was a provisional judgment (قضاء تقديري) before hearing the speech of the other litigant; for it is possible that the other had something to say that would reveal him to be justified in what he requested and proposed to his companion. However, the owner of the single ewe presented his speech in a way that stirred mercy and compassion in him (عليه‌السلام), so he hastened to this provisional confirmation, saying: "He has certainly wronged you by asking for your ewe [to add it] to his ewes."

The "L" (اللام) is for an oath (القسم). The "asking" (السؤال)—as has been said—incorporates the meaning of "adding" (الإضافة), which is why it was made transitive to the second object with "to" (إلى). The meaning is: I swear, he has certainly wronged you by asking to add your ewe to his ewes.

His saying: وَإِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْخُلَطاءِ لَيَبْغِي بَعْضُهمْ عَلى بَعْضٍ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَقَلِيلٌ ما هُمْ — "‘Indeed many partners wrong one another, except those who have faith and do righteous deeds, and they are few.’" (38:24). This is part of David’s (عليه‌السلام) speech, by which he confirms his first statement. "Partners" (الخلطاء) are associates who are intermingled in business.

His saying, the Exalted: وَظَنَّ داوُدُ أَنَّما فَتَنَّاهُ فَاسْتَغْفَرَ رَبَّهُ وَخَرَّ راكِعاً وَأَنابَ — "David realized that We had only tested him, so he pleaded with his Lord for forgiveness, and fell down bowing and repented." (38:24). That is, David knew that We had only tested him with this event; that it was merely a "trial" (فتنة) — a divine test or tribulation — by which We tested him. It has been said that "realized" (ظن) here carries its common meaning, which is the opposite of certainty (اليقين); however, the mention of his seeking forgiveness and his repentance in absolute terms supports what we have presented. If "realized" carried its common meaning, the seeking of forgiveness and repentance would be conditional upon it actually being a trial, but the absolute nature of the wording refutes this. "Falling down" (الخر), as mentioned by Al-Raghib, is a fall from which a sound (خرير) is heard; "sound" (الخرير) is used for the sound of water, wind, and other things that fall from a height. "Bowing" (الركوع), as he mentioned, is any form of inclining.

"Repentance" (الإنابة) to Allah—as mentioned by Al-Raghib—is returning to Him with penitence and sincerity of action; it is from "turns" (النوب), meaning the return of a thing time after time.

The meaning is: David knew that this event was only a test We had put him through, and that he had erred, so he sought forgiveness from his Lord—for what had occurred from him—and fell down inclining and turned back to Him.

Most commentators, following the narrations, hold that these litigants who entered upon David (عليه‌السلام) were angels sent by Allah the Glorified to him to test him; you will soon learn the status of these narrations.

However, the specific details of the story—such as their climbing the wall of the sanctuary and their entering upon him in an unusual manner such that they panicked him; likewise, his realization that it was only a trial from Allah for him and not an ordinary event; and the Exalted’s saying afterward: فاحْكُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِالْحَقِّ وَلا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوى (So judge between the people with truth and do not follow desire)—all clearly indicate that Allah afflicted him with what He afflicted him with to alert him and guide him in his vicegerency (الخلافة) and his judgment among the people. All this supports their being from the angels, having manifested to him in the form of human men.

Accordingly, the event was a manifestation (تمثل) in which the angels appeared in the form of two litigants, one of whom had a single ewe which another, who had ninety-nine ewes, was asking for. They asked him for a judgment, and he said to the owner of the single ewe: "He has certainly wronged you..." etc. His (عليه‌السلام) statement—had it been a final judgment—would have been a judgment within the realm of manifestation (ظرف التمثل), just as if he had seen them in a dream and said to them what he said and judged among them as he judged. It is well known that there is no religious obligation (تكليف) in the realm of manifestation, just as there is no obligation in the world of dreams. Obligation only exists in our witnessed world, which is the world of matter (عالم المادة). The event did not occur in it, nor were there two litigants or ewes except in the realm of manifestation. Thus, David’s (عليه‌السلام) "error" was in this realm of manifestation where there is no obligation, similar to Adam’s (عليه‌السلام) error in Paradise by eating from the tree before the descent to Earth and the legislation of laws and the establishment of obligations. His seeking forgiveness and his repentance for what issued from him were like Adam’s seeking forgiveness and repentance for what issued from him. Allah explicitly mentioned his vicegerency in His speech, just as He explicitly mentioned Adam’s (عليه‌السلام) vicegerency in His speech; the explanation of this has already passed in the story of Adam (عليه‌السلام) in Surah al-Baqarah in the first volume of this book.

As for the view of some commentators that the litigants who entered upon him were human beings and the story is to be taken literally, then his saying: "He has certainly wronged you..." etc., should be taken as a provisional judgment; i.e., you are wronged if your opponent does not bring a clear proof. This is to preserve what has been established by both reason and tradition: that the prophets are protected by the "inerrancy" (العصمة) — the divine protection from sin and error — of Allah, and that neither major nor minor sins are permissible for them.

Furthermore, Allah the Glorified explicitly stated previously that He gave him wisdom and decisive judgment, and this does not harmonize with his making an error in judgment.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ لَهُ عِنْدَنا لَزُلْفى وَحُسْنَ مَآبٍ — "Indeed he has a near station with Us and a good destination." (38:25). "Near station" (الزلفة والزلفى) refers to rank and favor, and "destination" (المآب) refers to the place of return. The indefiniteness of "near station" (لَزُلْفى) and "destination" (مَآبٍ) is for magnification (التفخيم). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: يا داوُدُ إِنَّا جَعَلْناكَ خَلِيفَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ — "O David! Indeed We have made you a vicegerent on the earth..." (38:26) to the end of the verse. It appears that the speech assumes a preceding statement; the estimation being: So We forgave him that and said, "O David..." etc.

The apparent meaning of "vicegerency" (الخلافة) — the status of being a representative or successor — is that it is the vicegerency of Allah; thus it corresponds to what is in His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً — "When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a vicegerent on the earth.’" (2:30). It is the nature of vicegerency that the vicegerent emulates the One who appointed him in His attributes and actions. Thus, the vicegerent of Allah on earth must adopt the character of Allah, will and act as Allah wills, and judge and decree as Allah decrees—and Allah decrees with truth—and follow the path of Allah without deviating from it.

For this reason, He branched off from the establishment of his vicegerency His saying: فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِالْحَقِّ — "so judge between the people with truth." This supports the idea that the intended meaning of establishing his vicegerency is bringing it from potentiality into actuality in his regard, not merely a potential vicegerency, because Allah perfected him in his attributes and gave him the kingdom to judge among the people.

The view of some—that the intended vicegerency is his succeeding the prophets before him, and that the branching of His saying "so judge between the people with truth" is because vicegerency is a great blessing whose gratitude is justice, or that what follows is the absolute judgment among people which is an effect of vicegerency, and its restriction to truth is because its correctness lies therein—is an interpretation of the wording without evidence.

His saying: وَلا تَتَّبِعِ الْهَوى فَيُضِلَّكَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ — "and do not follow desire, or it will lead you astray from the way of Allah." (38:26). The coordination and contrast between this and what preceded it indicate that the meaning is: Do not follow in your judgment "desire" (الهوى) — the whims and inclinations of the self — for it will lead you astray from the truth, which is the way of Allah. Thus, the verse implies that the way of Allah is the truth.

Some have said: In His commanding him (عليه‌السلام) to judge with truth and His forbidding him from following desire, there is an admonition for others who take charge of people's affairs to judge among them with truth and not follow falsehood; otherwise, he (عليه‌السلام), by virtue of being inerrant (معصوم), does not judge except with truth and does not follow falsehood.

The response to this is that the matter of admonishing others through the obligation directed at him is appropriate; however, the inerrancy of the inerrant and his not judging except with truth does not prevent the direction of obligation through command and prohibition toward him. Inerrancy does not entail the stripping of his free will (الاختيار), and as long as his free will remains, it is permissible—rather, necessary—for obligation to be directed toward him just as it is directed toward other people. Were it not for the direction of obligation toward the inerrant, no "obligatory" (واجب) or "prohibited" (محرم) would be realized in his regard, and obedience would not be distinguished from disobedience; thus, the meaning of inerrancy—which is being protected from disobedience—would become void.

His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ — "Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because they forgot the Day of Reckoning." (38:26). This is a justification for the prohibition of following desire, on the grounds that it necessitates forgetting the Day of Reckoning, and in its forgetting is a severe punishment. What is meant by "forgetting" it is the lack of concern for its matter.

In the verse is an indication that every straying from the way of Allah the Glorified through any act of disobedience is inseparable from forgetting the Day of Reckoning.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما باطِلاً — "We did not create the heaven and the earth and whatever is between them in vain." (38:27) to the end of the verse. When the speech concluded with the mention of the Day of Reckoning, He turned the course of the explanation toward it, arguing for it with two proofs. One of them is what He presented in this verse by His saying: "We did not create the heaven..." etc. This is an argument from the perspective of "ends" (الغايات); for if the creation of the heaven and the earth and what is between them—which are created, temporal things that come into being and perish—did not lead to a fixed, eternal, and non-temporal end, it would be "in vain" (باطلاً). That which is "in vain"—meaning that which has no end—is impossible to be realized in external reality. Furthermore, it is impossible for the Wise One, and there is no doubt in His wisdom, the Exalted.

Sometimes "in vain" (الباطل) is used to mean "play" (اللعب). If that were the intended meaning, the verse would be in the sense of His saying: وَما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ — "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. We did not create them except with truth." (44:38-39).

It has been said: The verse is coordinated with what preceded it in terms of meaning, as if it were said: Do not follow desire because it will be a cause for your straying, and because He, the Exalted, did not create the world for the sake of following desire—which is vanity—but rather created it for monotheism (التوحيد) and following the Law (الشرع).

The response is that the following verse: أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ (Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth...) etc., does not harmonize with this meaning.

His saying: ذلِكَ ظَنُّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَوَيْلٌ لِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنَ النَّارِ — "That is the opinion of those who disbelieve. So woe to the disbelievers from the Fire!" (38:27). That is, the creation of the world in vain without an end, and the negation of the Day of Reckoning in which the outcome of the reckoning of affairs appears, is the "opinion" (ظن) of those who disbelieve in the Resurrection; so woe to them from the punishment of the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ — "Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?" (38:28). This is the second proof for the Resurrection. Its formulation is that for the human being, like all other species, there is necessarily a perfection (كمال). The perfection of the human being is their transition in the two aspects of knowledge and action from potentiality to actuality, by believing in true beliefs and performing righteous deeds to which their sound "primordial nature" (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — guides them. These are faith in the truth and righteous action, by which the human society on earth is rectified.

Thus, those who have faith and do righteous deeds—and they are the "God-conscious" (المتقون) — those who are wary of Allah's displeasure and maintain piety — are the perfected among human beings. Those who cause corruption on the earth through the corruption of their belief and action—and they are the "vicious" (الفجار) — those who openly transgress divine limits — are the deficient ones, truly losing their humanity. The requirement of this perfection and deficiency is that there be, in exchange for perfection, a happy life and a pleasant existence, and in exchange for its opposite, the opposite of that.

It is well known that this worldly life in which they both share is under the control of material causes and factors, and its relation to the perfected and the deficient, the believer and the disbeliever, is equal. Whoever performs well and is favored by material causes attains a pleasant life, and whoever is otherwise is beset by misery and a constricted life.

So, if life were restricted to this worldly life, whose relation to both groups is equal, and there were no life specific to each of them that suited their state, that would contradict the Divine Care (العناية الإلهية) in delivering to every possessor of a right their right and giving to requirements what they require.

If you wish, you could say: Treating both groups equally (1) and nullifying what the rectitude of the former and the corruption of the latter require is contrary to His justice, the Exalted.

The verse—as you see—does not merely negate the equality of the state of the believer and the disbeliever; rather, it established the contrast between those who have faith and do righteous deeds and those who are not so, whether they be non-believers or non-righteous believers. For this reason, it presented the contrast a second time between the God-conscious and the vicious.

His saying, the Exalted: كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ مُبارَكٌ لِيَدَّبَّرُوا آياتِهِ وَلِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ — "[This is] a blessed Book that We have sent down to you, so that they may contemplate its signs, and that those who possess intellect may take admonition." (38:29). That is, this is a Book whose description is such and such. Its description with "sending down" (الإنزال) — the act of revealing as a whole — which suggests a single instance, rather than "gradual revelation" (التنزيل) which indicates succession, is because the "contemplation" (التدبر) and "admonition" (التذكر) mentioned are suited to considering it as a whole, not as scattered parts.

The contrast between "so that they may contemplate" (لِيَدَّبَّرُوا) and "that those who possess intellect may take admonition" (لِيَتَذَكَّرَ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ) indicates that the intended meaning of the plural pronoun is people in general.

The meaning is: This is a Book We have sent down to you, abundant in goodness and blessings for the commonality and the elite, so that people may contemplate it and thus be guided by it or so that the proof may be completed against them; and so that "those who possess intellect" (أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ) — those endowed with pure and sound reason — may take admonition by it, thus being guided to the truth by summoning its proof and receiving it from its explanation.

(1) The first proof is demonstrative (برهانية) and the second is dialectical (جدلية).

(Discussion Regarding Narrations)

It is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur, through a chain from Anas, from Mujahid, and al-Suddi, and through several chains from Ibn Abbas, the story of the litigants entering upon David (عليه‌السلام), with variations in the narrations. Al-Qummi narrated the like of it in his Tafsir, and it was narrated in al-Ara'is and others. It was summarized in Majma' al-Bayan as follows: David used to pray much, and he said: "O Lord, You favored Abraham over me and took him as a friend; and You favored Moses over me and spoke to him directly." He said: "O David, We tested them with that which We have not tested you with the like of; if you wish, I shall test you." He said: "Yes, O Lord, test me."

While he was in his sanctuary one day, a pigeon landed. He wanted to take it, but it flew to the window of the sanctuary. He went to take it and looked out from the window, and there was the wife of Uriah son of Hannan bathing. He desired her and intended to marry her. So he sent Uriah to one of his military detachments and ordered that he be placed in front of the Ark (التابوت) in which was the "Tranquility" (السكينة). This was done, and he was killed.

When her waiting period (العدة) ended, he married her and went in unto her, and Solomon was born to him from her. While he was in his sanctuary one day, two men entered upon him, and he was panicked by them. They said: "Do not be afraid. [We are] two litigants, one of whom has wronged the other..." until His saying: "and they are few." One of the two men looked at his companion and laughed. David then realized that they were two angels whom Allah had sent to him in the form of two litigants to rebuke him for his error. So he repented and wept until plants grew from the abundance of his tears.

Then it is said in al-Majma'—and how well it was said: "This is among that whose corruption is beyond doubt, for such things impugn justice (العدالة). How could it be permissible for the prophets of Allah—who are His trustees over His revelation and His envoys between Him and His creation—to be described as those whose testimony would not be accepted, and to be in a state that causes aversion to listening to them and accepting from them?"

I say: The story is taken from the Torah, except that what is in it is more hideous and atrocious, so it was modified somewhat, as will become clear to you.

In the Torah, the summary is: It happened in the evening that David arose from his bed and walked on the roof of the king's house. From the roof, he saw a woman bathing, and the woman was very beautiful to look at.

David sent and inquired about the woman, and it was said: "Is she not Bathsheba, the wife of Uriah the Hittite?" David sent messengers and took her, and she came to him, and he lay with her—she being purified from her uncleanness. Then she returned to her house and the woman conceived. She sent and told David that she was pregnant.

Uriah was in David's army fighting the children of Ammon. David wrote to Joab, the commander of his army, ordering him to send Uriah to him. When he came and stayed with him for some days, he wrote a letter to Joab (1) and sent it by the hand of Uriah. In the letter he wrote, saying: "Set Uriah in the forefront of the hottest battle, and retreat from him, that he may be struck down and die." This was done to him, and he was killed, and David was informed of it.

When the wife of Uriah heard that he was dead, she mourned for her husband. When the mourning was over, David sent and brought her to his house, and she became his wife and bore him a son. But the thing that David had done was evil in the eyes of the Lord.

So the Lord sent Nathan the prophet to David. He came to him and said to him: "There were two men in one city, one rich and the other poor. The rich man had very many flocks and herds. But the poor man had nothing except one little ewe lamb, which he had bought and nourished. A traveler came to the rich man, and he was unwilling to take from his own flock and herd to prepare for the traveler who had come to him; but he took the poor man's ewe and prepared it for his guest." David's anger was greatly kindled against the man, and he said to Nathan: "As the Lord lives, the man who has done this deserves to die! And he shall restore the ewe fourfold, because he did this thing and because he had no pity."

Nathan said to David: "You are the man! The Lord rebukes you and says: 'I will raise up evil against you out of your own house; and I will take your wives before your eyes and give them to your neighbor, and he shall lie with your wives in the sight of all Israel and before the sun—as a recompense for what you did to Uriah and his wife.'"

David said to Nathan: "I have sinned against the Lord." Nathan said to David: "The Lord also has put away your sin; you shall not die. Nevertheless, because by this deed you have given great occasion to the enemies of the Lord to blaspheme, the child who is born to you shall surely die." So Allah made the boy sick for seven days and then took him. Then the wife of Uriah bore to David his son Solomon after him.

In al-Uyun, in the chapter on the assembly of al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) with al-Ma'mun and the leaders of religions and sects: Imam al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) said to Ibn Jahm: "As for David, what do those on your side say about him?" He said: "They say that David was praying in his sanctuary when Iblis appeared to him in the form of a bird, the most beautiful of birds. David cut off his prayer and stood up to take the bird into the house. He went out after it, and the bird flew to the roof. He climbed in pursuit of it, and the bird fell into the house of Uriah son of Hannan.

David looked out after the bird and saw Uriah's wife bathing. When he looked at her, he desired her. He had sent Uriah on one of his expeditions, so he wrote to his commander: 'Place Uriah in front of the Ark.' He was placed there, and Uriah was victorious over the polytheists, which was difficult for David. So he wrote to him a second time: 'Place him in front of the Ark.' He was placed there and Uriah was killed, and David married his wife."

He said: Imam al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) struck his hand against his forehead and said: "Indeed we belong to Allah and to Him we shall return! You have attributed to a prophet from the prophets of Allah a disregard for his prayer such that he went out after a bird, then an indecency, then a murder!"

He asked: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, what then was his error?" He replied: "Woe to you! David (عليه‌السلام) only thought that Allah had not created a creature more knowledgeable than himself. So Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent to him the two angels, and they climbed the wall of the sanctuary and said: '[We are] two litigants, one of whom has wronged the other; so judge between us with truth and do not be unjust, and guide us to the even path. Indeed this is my brother; he has ninety-nine ewes and I have a single ewe. Then he said, ‘‘Entrust it to me,’’ and he overpowered me in speech.' David hastened against the defendant and said: 'He has certainly wronged you by asking for your ewe [to add it] to his ewes.' He did not ask the claimant for proof of that, nor did he turn to the defendant and say to him: 'What do you say?' This was an error in the protocol of judgment (رسم الحكم), not what you have gone toward. Do you not hear Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, saying: يا داوُدُ إِنَّا جَعَلْناكَ خَلِيفَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِالْحَقِّ to the end of the verse?"

He asked: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, what is his story with Uriah?" Imam al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) said: "In the days of David, if a woman's husband died or was killed, she would never marry after him. The first person for whom Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, made it permissible to marry a woman whose husband was killed was David (عليه‌السلام). So he married Uriah's wife when he was killed and her waiting period ended. That is what was difficult for the people regarding the killing of Uriah."

In Amali al-Saduq, with its chain to Abu Abdillah (عليه‌السلام): He said to Alqamah: "The pleasure of people cannot be possessed and their tongues cannot be controlled. Did they not attribute to David (عليه‌السلام) that he followed a bird until he looked at Uriah's wife and desired her, and that he placed her husband in front of the Ark until he was killed, then married her?" (The hadith).

(1) Summarized from the eleventh and twelfth chapters of Second Samuel.

(A Discussion on the Accounts of David in Sections)

1 - His story in the Quran: Nothing has occurred of his story in the Quran except indications. The Glorified One mentioned that he was in the army of King Saul (طالوت) when he fought Goliath, and David killed him. So Allah gave him the kingdom after Saul, and wisdom, and taught him of what He willed (Baqarah: 251). He made him a vicegerent for Himself to judge among the people and gave him decisive judgment (Sad: 20 and 26). Allah strengthened his kingdom and disposed the mountains and birds with him to glorify along with him (al-Anbiya: 79, Sad: 19). He made iron soft for him to work with and weave coats of mail from it (al-Anbiya: 80, Saba: 11).

2 - The beautiful praise for him in the Quran: The Glorified One counted him among the prophets and praised him with what He praised them with. He singled him out with His saying: وَآتَيْنا داوُدَ زَبُوراً — "and We gave David the Psalms." (4:163). He gave him favor and knowledge (Saba: 10, al-Naml: 15). He gave him wisdom and decisive judgment and made him a vicegerent on the earth (Sad: 20 and 26). He described him as being penitent (أواب) and that he has a near station with Him and a good destination (Sad: 19 and 25).

3 - Contemplation of the verses of the Book addressing the story of the litigants entering upon David (عليه‌السلام) does not yield more than its being a trial from Him, the Exalted, for him (عليه‌السلام) in the realm of manifestation (ظرف التمثل), in order to nurture him with a divine nurturing and teach him the protocol of just judgment, so that he does not transgress in judgment nor deviate from justice.

As for what most narrations contain regarding the story of Uriah and his wife, it is something from which the prophets are far removed and their presence is purified. The summary of the discussion on that has already passed in the explanation of the verses and the discussion regarding narrations.

Ayahs

وَوَهَبْنَا لِدَاوُۥدَ سُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۚ نِعْمَ ٱلْعَبْدُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥٓ أَوَّابٌ (٣٠)
And to David We gave Solomon—what an excellent servant he was! Indeed, he was a penitent [soul]. (30)
إِذْ عُرِضَ عَلَيْهِ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ ٱلصَّـٰفِنَـٰتُ ٱلْجِيَادُ (٣١)
One evening when there were displayed before him prancing steeds, (31)
فَقَالَ إِنِّىٓ أَحْبَبْتُ حُبَّ ٱلْخَيْرِ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّى حَتَّىٰ تَوَارَتْ بِٱلْحِجَابِ (٣٢)
he said, ‘Indeed I have preferred the love of [worldly] niceties to the remembrance of my Lord until [the sun] disappeared behind the [night’s] veil.’ (32)
رُدُّوهَا عَلَىَّ ۖ فَطَفِقَ مَسْحًۢا بِٱلسُّوقِ وَٱلْأَعْنَاقِ (٣٣)
‘Bring it back for me!’ Then he [and others] began to wipe [their] legs and necks. (33)
وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا سُلَيْمَـٰنَ وَأَلْقَيْنَا عَلَىٰ كُرْسِيِّهِۦ جَسَدًا ثُمَّ أَنَابَ (٣٤)
Certainly We tried Solomon, and cast a [lifeless] body on his throne. Thereupon he was penitent. (34)
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱغْفِرْ لِى وَهَبْ لِى مُلْكًا لَّا يَنۢبَغِى لِأَحَدٍ مِّنۢ بَعْدِىٓ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْوَهَّابُ (٣٥)
He said, ‘My Lord! Forgive me, and grant me a kingdom that will not befit anyone except me. Indeed You are the All-munificent.’ (35)
فَسَخَّرْنَا لَهُ ٱلرِّيحَ تَجْرِى بِأَمْرِهِۦ رُخَآءً حَيْثُ أَصَابَ (٣٦)
So We disposed the wind for him, blowing softly wherever he intended by his command, (36)
وَٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ كُلَّ بَنَّآءٍ وَغَوَّاصٍ (٣٧)
and every builder and diver from the demons, (37)
وَءَاخَرِينَ مُقَرَّنِينَ فِى ٱلْأَصْفَادِ (٣٨)
and others [too] bound together in chains. (38)
هَـٰذَا عَطَآؤُنَا فَٱمْنُنْ أَوْ أَمْسِكْ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (٣٩)
‘This is Our bounty: so withhold or bestow without any reckoning.’ (39)
وَإِنَّ لَهُۥ عِندَنَا لَزُلْفَىٰ وَحُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ (٤٠)
Indeed he has [a station of] nearness with Us and a good destination. (40)

(Commentary)

This is the second of the stories concerning the penitent servants (العباد الأوابين) which the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded to bear with patience and to remember.

His saying, the Exalted: وَوَهَبْنَا لِدَاوُودَ سُلَيْمَانَ نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ "And We gave Solomon to David. What an excellent servant! Indeed he was a penitent [soul]" (38:30). That is, We granted him Solomon as a son; the remainder of the verse is clear from what has preceded.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ عُرِضَ عَلَيْهِ بِالْعَشِيِّ الصَّافِنَاتُ الْجِيَادُ "When one evening there were displayed before him the prancing steeds" (38:31). The evening (العشي) is the opposite of the morning (الغداة), referring to the end of the day after the sun begins its decline (الزوال). According to al-Majma‘, the prancing steeds (الصافنات) is the plural of ṣāfinah (الصافنة), referring to horses that stand on three legs and raise one of their forelegs until it rests on the edge of the hoof. He said: the steeds (الجياد) is the plural of jawād (جواد); the yā’ here is transformed from a wāw, the root being jawād (جواد), referring to swift horses, as if they "generously give" (تجود) of their speed in running. End of quote.

His saying, the Exalted: فَقَالَ إِنِّي أَحْبَبْتُ حُبَّ الْخَيْرِ عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّي حَتَّى تَوارَتْ بِالْحِجَابِ "He said, ‘Indeed I have preferred the love of [worldly] goods above the remembrance of my Lord, until [the sun] disappeared behind the veil [of night]’" (38:32). The pronoun refers to Solomon. It is said that "the goods" (الخير) refers to horses, as the Arabs call horses khayr (goods/wealth). It is narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "Good (الخير) is tied to the forelocks of horses until the Day of Resurrection."

It is also said that "the goods" (الخير) refers to abundant wealth, and it has been used with this meaning in several places in the speech of the Exalted, such as His saying: إِنْ تَرَكَ خَيْراً "...if he leaves any wealth" (2:180).

Regarding His saying: إِنِّي أَحْبَبْتُ حُبَّ الْخَيْرِ عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّي "Indeed I have preferred the love of [worldly] goods above the remembrance of my Lord," the commentators say that "I have loved" (أَحْبَبْتُ) implies the meaning of "preferring" (الإيثار), and the preposition "from/above" (عَنْ) carries the meaning of "over" (على). The meaning would be: "I have preferred the love of horses over the remembrance of my Lord"—which is prayer (الصلاة)—while loving them; or "I loved the horses with a love that preferred them over the remembrance of my Lord." Thus, he became occupied with the display of horses presented to him, neglecting the prayer until the sun set.

Regarding His saying: حَتَّى تَوارَتْ بِالْحِجَابِ "until [the sun] disappeared behind the veil," they say the pronoun refers to the sun, and its "disappearing behind the veil" refers to its setting and being hidden behind the veil of the horizon. This meaning is supported by the mention of "the evening" (العشي) in the previous verse, for without this, there would be no apparent purpose served by mentioning the evening.

The summary of the verse's meaning is: "The love of horses—when they were displayed before me—distracted me from the prayer until its time passed with the setting of the sun." He only loved the horses for the sake of Allah, to prepare for struggle in the way of Allah (الجهاد في سبيل الله); thus, attending the display was itself an act of worship (عبادة) for him. Consequently, one act of worship distracted him from another, except that he considered the prayer to be more important.

It is also said that the pronoun in "disappeared" (تَوارَتْ) refers to the horses. This would mean that he ordered the horses to be run, and watching them run distracted him until they vanished from his sight and were hidden by the veil of distance. However, it has already been mentioned that the reference to "the evening" supports the previous meaning, and there is no evidence in the wording of the verse for the story of ordering them to run.

His saying, the Exalted: رُدُّوهَا عَلَيَّ فَطَفِقَ مَسْحاً بِالسُّوقِ وَالْأَعْنَاِق "‘Bring them back to me!’ Then he began to pass his hand over [their] legs and necks" (38:33). It is said that the pronoun in "Bring them back" (رُدُّوهَا) refers to the sun; this would be his command to the angels to bring back the sun so he could perform his prayer in its time. His saying: فَطَفِقَ مَسْحاً بِالسُّوقِ وَالْأَعْنَاِق "Then he began to pass his hand over [their] legs and necks" would mean he began wiping his own shanks and neck and commanded his companions to wipe their shanks and necks, which was their form of ablution (الوضوء); then he and they prayed. This has been mentioned in some narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام).

It is also said that the pronoun refers to the horses. The meaning would be: He said, "Bring back the horses," and when they were brought back, he began stroking their shanks and necks, dedicating them to the way of Allah as a compensation for being distracted by them from the prayer.

It is further said that the pronoun refers to the horses, but "wiping" (مسح) their necks and shanks means striking them with the sword and severing them, as "wiping" can mean cutting. According to this view, he (peace be upon him) became angry with them for the sake of Allah because they distracted him from the remembrance of Allah; so he ordered them brought back and then struck their necks and shanks with the sword, killing them all.

However, such an action is something from which the station of the Prophets (عليه‌السلام) is transcendent. What was the sin of the horses if looking at them distracted him from prayer, such that they should be punished with the severest of punishments and slaughtered to the last one in such a gruesome manner, especially considering the destruction of valuable property (إتلاف المال المحترم)?

As for the argument some make based on a narration from Ubayy ibn Ka‘b from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding the verse: فَطَفِقَ مَسْحاً بِالسُّوقِ وَالْأَعْنَاِق—that he cut their shanks and necks with the sword—and then added that he made them an offering (قربانا) to Allah, and that the offering of horses was permissible in his law: there is no mention of "offering" in the hadith nor elsewhere.

Furthermore, he (peace be upon him) was not distracted from worship by idle desire (الهوى); rather, one worship distracted him from another, as previously indicated.

Therefore, the most reliable view is the first one, provided the wording of the verse supports it; otherwise, it is the second view.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا سُلَيْمَانَ وَأَلْقَيْنَا عَلَى كُرْسِيِّهِ جَسَداً ثُمَّ أَنَابَ "Certainly We tried Solomon, and cast a [lifeless] body on his throne. Then he turned [to Us] penitently" (38:34). The body (الجسد) is a physical form without a soul.

It is said that the "body" cast upon his throne was Solomon himself, due to an illness with which Allah tested him. The implied meaning would be: "We cast him upon his throne as a body," i.e., like a body without a soul due to the severity of the illness.

However, the omission of the pronoun from "We cast him" and the presentation of the speech in its current form—which clearly indicates that the thing cast was "the body"—undermines the intended meaning. The most eloquent of speech (أفصح الكلام) cannot be interpreted in such a way.

Other commentators have various opinions regarding the meaning of the verse, following different narrations. What can be summarized from them is that it was the body of a son of his whom Allah caused to die and whose body was cast upon his throne. His saying: ثُمَّ أَنَابَ قَالَ رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي "Then he turned [to Us] penitently. He said, ‘My Lord! Forgive me...’" suggests or indicates that he (peace be upon him) had some hope or wish regarding this child in relation to Allah; so Allah, the Glorified, caused him to die and cast him upon his throne to alert him to delegate all matters to Allah and submit to Him.

His saying: قَالَ رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَهَبْ لِي مُلْكاً لا يَنْبَغِي لِأَحَدٍ مِنْ بَعْدِي إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْوَهَّابُ "He said, ‘My Lord! Forgive me, and grant me a kingdom such as shall not befit anyone after me. Indeed You are the All-munificent’" (38:35). The apparent context is that the seeking of forgiveness is linked to what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding the casting of the body on the throne. The separation of the sentences is because the speech is in the position of answering an implied question; as if when it was said, "Then he turned penitently," it was asked: "What did he say?" and the reply was: "He said, ‘My Lord! Forgive me...’" etc.
