A problem is sometimes raised regarding his saying: "and grant me a kingdom such as shall not befit anyone after me," suggesting it implies stinginess or miserliness, as it stipulates that no one else in the worlds should be given a kingdom like his.

This is refuted by the fact that he was asking for a kingdom unique to him (ملك يختص به), not asking that others be prevented from receiving what he was given or be deprived of it. There is a difference between asking for a unique kingdom and asking for the exclusivity of a kingdom he was given.

His saying, the Exalted: فَسَخَّرْنَا لَهُ الرِّيحَ تَجْرِي بِأَمْرِهِ رُخَاءً حَيْثُ أَصَابَ "So We disposed the wind for him, blowing softly by his command wherever he intended" (38:36). This follows from his request for the kingdom and informs of the answering of his prayer, explaining the kingdom that would not befit anyone else: the subjugation of the wind and the jinn.

Softness (الرخاء) refers to that which is gentle. It appears that the wind "blowing softly by his command" means its compliance with his order and the ease of its flow according to his will (peace be upon him). Thus, describing the wind here as "soft" (رخاء) does not contradict its description in His saying: وَلِسُلَيْمَانَ الرِّيحَ عَاصِفَةً تَجْرِي بِأَمْرِهِ "And [We disposed] for Solomon the tempestuous wind, blowing by his command..." (21:81) as being "tempestuous" (عاصفة).

It has been answered that it is possible Allah made it soft at times and tempestuous at others, according to what Solomon (peace be upon him) desired.

His saying: حَيْثُ أَصَابَ "wherever he intended" means wherever Solomon (peace be upon him) wished and aimed; it is connected to "blowing."

His saying, the Exalted: وَالشَّيَاطِينَ كُلَّ بَنَّاءٍ وَغَوَّاصٍ "and the devils, every builder and diver" (38:37). That is, We subjugated for him the devils from among the jinn: every builder among them who built for him on land, and every diver who worked for him in the sea to extract pearls and other things.

His saying, the Exalted: وَآخَرِينَ مُقَرَّنِينَ فِي الْأَصْفَادِ "and others bound together in shackles" (38:38). Shackles (الأصفاد) is the plural of ṣafad (صفد), which is an iron collar. The meaning is: We subjugated for him others from among them, gathered in collars and bound with chains.

His saying, the Exalted: هَذَا عَطَاؤُنَا فَامْنُنْ أَوْ أَمْسِكْ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ "‘This is Our gift: so bestow or withhold without any reckoning’" (38:39). That is, this mentioned kingdom is Our gift to you without reckoning. It appears that "without reckoning" (بغير حساب) means it is not exhausted by giving or withholding; hence it was said: "so bestow or withhold," meaning both are equal in that they do not affect the abundance of the gift.

It is also said that "without reckoning" means you will not be called to account for it on the Day of Resurrection. Others say it means the giving is a favor (تفضل), not a recompense (مجازاة), among other interpretations.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ لَهُ عِنْدَنَا لَزُلْفَى وَحُسْنَ مَآبٍ "Indeed he has a near station with Us and a good destination" (38:40). Its meaning has been explained previously.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying: فَقَالَ إِنِّي أَحْبَبْتُ حُبَّ الْخَيْرِ عَنْ ذِكْرِ رَبِّي—the verse—it is said: These horses distracted him from the afternoon prayer (صلاة العصر) until its time passed. This is narrated from Ali (عليه‌السلام), and in the narration of our companions (the Shi‘a), it is said that he missed the beginning of its time.

In the same source, Ibn Abbas said: I asked Ali about this verse, and he said: "What have you heard regarding it, O Ibn Abbas?" I said: "I heard Ka‘b saying: Solomon was occupied with the display of horses until the prayer passed him by. Then he said: ‘Bring them back to me’—meaning the horses, which were fourteen—and he ordered their shanks and necks to be struck with the sword and killed them. Consequently, Allah stripped him of his kingdom for fourteen days because he wronged the horses by killing them."

Ali said: "Ka‘b lied. Rather, Solomon was occupied with the display of horses one day because he intended to struggle against the enemy (جهاد العدو), until the sun disappeared behind the veil. Then, by the command of Allah, he said to the angels entrusted with the sun: ‘Bring it back to me.’ So it was brought back, and he performed the afternoon prayer in its time. Indeed, the Prophets of Allah do not commit injustice nor command it, for they are infallible (معصومون) and purified (مطهرون)."

I say: The statement of Ka‘b al-Ahbar, "Allah stripped him of his kingdom," is an allusion to the story of the ring, which we shall point to.

In al-Faqih, it is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) that he said: "One day in the evening, horses were displayed before Solomon ibn David. He became occupied with looking at them until the sun disappeared behind the veil. He said to the angels: ‘Bring back the sun to me so that I may perform my prayer in its time.’ They brought it back, so he stood and wiped his shanks and neck in such a manner—and that was their ablution (وضوءهم) for prayer. Then he stood and prayed. When he finished, the sun set and the stars appeared. That is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: وَوَهَبْنَا لِدَاوُودَ سُلَيْمَانَ—until His saying—مَسْحاً بِالسُّوقِ وَالْأَعْنَاِق."

I say: There is no problem with this narration if the wording of the verse—namely His saying: فَطَفِقَ مَسْحاً بِالسُّوقِ وَالْأَعْنَاِق—supports the meaning contained within it. As for the issue of the "returning of the sun" (رد الشمس), there is no impossibility in it once the miracles of the Prophets (إعجاز الأنبياء) are established. The returning of the sun has been narrated for others besides him (peace be upon him), such as Joshua ibn Nun and Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه‌السلام) in reliable reports; no weight is given to the objections raised by al-Razi in his Tafsir al-Kabir.

As for his hamstringing the horses and striking their necks with the sword, several narrations have been reported regarding this through Sunni channels, and al-Qummi included it in his Tafsir. It seems these narrations trace back to Ka‘b, as seen in the previously mentioned report of Ibn Abbas. In any case, no weight is given to them, as stated before.

The extent of their exaggeration in this story reached the point where they narrated that the horses numbered twenty thousand winged steeds. Similar to this is what was narrated regarding His saying: حَتَّى تَوارَتْ بِالْحِجَابِ from Ka‘b, that the "veil" is a green ruby surrounding all creation, from which the sky derives its green color.

Such narrations are among the wonders of stories they have narrated regarding His saying: وَأَلْقَيْنَا عَلَى كُرْسِيِّهِ جَسَداً.

For instance, it is narrated that a son was born to him, and he ordered that he be nursed and kept in the clouds out of compassion for him, fearing the rebellious jinn—and in some reports, fearing the Angel of Death—but one day his body fell upon his throne dead.

And it is narrated that he said one day: "Tonight I will surely go around to a hundred of my wives, and each one of them will bear me a horseman who will struggle in the way of Allah." He did not say, "If Allah wills" (the exception), so none of them conceived except one, who bore half a child. He loved him, and some jinn hid him from the Angel of Death, but the Angel took him from his hiding place and his soul was taken upon Solomon's throne.

And it is narrated in many reports, several of which trace back to Ibn Abbas—who explicitly states in some that he took them from Ka‘b—that Solomon's kingdom was in his ring. A devil snatched it from him, so his kingdom vanished, and the devil dominated his kingdom for several days. Then Allah returned the ring to him, and he returned to his former kingdom. They have included in this story matters from which the station of the Prophets (عليه‌السلام) should be purified even from mentioning, let alone attributing to them. They said that the devil sitting on Solomon's throne is what is meant by His saying: وَأَلْقَيْنَا عَلَى كُرْسِيِّهِ جَسَداً.

All of these are among the things to which no weight is given, as previously indicated; they are merely the playthings of the hands of fabrication (أيدي الوضع).

Ayahs

thoughtful

(Commentary)

This is the third story among those the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded to bear with patience and to recount. It is the story of the Prophet Job (أيوب), peace be upon him (عليه‌السلام), and the tribulation he was tested with, followed by Allah’s honoring him with restoration and bestowal. This is followed by the command to mention Abraham and five of his progeny from among the Prophets (عليهم‌السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ عَبْدَنا أَيُّوبَ إِذْ نادى رَبَّهُ أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الشَّيْطانُ بِنُصْبٍ وَعَذابٍ “And remember Our servant Job, when he called out to his Lord, ‘The Devil has visited me with hardship and torment’” (38:41). This was a supplication from him (عليه السلام) and a request for well-being, asking his Lord to remove the ill state that had befallen him. He did not explicitly state what he wanted or asked for, out of humility (تواضعاً) and self-abasement (تذللاً), except that his calling upon the Exalted with the word "my Lord" (ربي) suggests that he was calling Him due to a need.

Hardship (النصب) means exhaustion (التعب). His saying: "when he called out" (إِذْ نادى) etc., is a substitutional clause of inclusion (بدل اشتمال) for "Our servant" (عَبْدَنا) or "Job" (أَيُّوبَ). His saying: "that the Devil has visited me" (أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ) etc., is the narration of his call.

It is apparent from the subsequent verses that what he meant by "hardship and torment" was the ill state that befell his body and his family. This is what was mentioned regarding him (عليه السلام) in Surah al-Anbiya’ in his call: أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الضُّرُّ وَأَنْتَ أَرْحَمُ الرَّاحِمِينَ “Distress has befallen me, and You are the most merciful of the merciful” (21:83), based on the inclusion of "distress" (الضر) for the calamity in his person and his family. There is no mention in this Surah, nor in Surah al-Anbiya’, of the loss of his wealth, although the mention of wealth occurs in the narrations (الروايات).

It appears that the meaning of the Devil "visiting" or "touching" him with hardship and torment is the attribution of his hardship and torment to the Devil by way of some form of causality (السببية) and influence (التأثير), which is what appears from the narrations. Attributing illness and the like to the Devil does not contradict its attribution also to some ordinary, natural causes (الأسباب العادية الطبيعية), because the two causes are not horizontal (عرضيين) and mutually exclusive; rather, one is longitudinal (في طول الآخر) to the other. We have clarified this in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ أَنَّ أَهْلَ الْقُرى آمَنُوا وَاتَّقَوْا لَفَتَحْنا عَلَيْهِمْ بَرَكاتٍ مِنَ السَّماءِ “If the people of the towns had been faithful and God-conscious, We would have opened to them blessings from the heaven” (7:96) in the eighth volume of this book.

There is no evidence indicating the impossibility of this type of influence by the Devil occurring in a human being. The Exalted has said: إِنَّمَا الْخَمْرُ وَالْمَيْسِرُ وَالْأَنْصابُ وَالْأَزْلامُ رِجْسٌ مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ “Wine, gambling, idols, and divining arrows are only an abomination, a work of Satan” (5:90), thus attributing these things themselves to him. He also said, narrating from Moses (عليه السلام): هذا مِنْ عَمَلِ الشَّيْطانِ إِنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ مُضِلٌّ مُبِينٌ “This is of Satan’s doing. Indeed he is an enemy, a manifest misleader” (28:15), referring to the fighting.

Even if one were to overlook the narrations, it would be possible to assume that the meaning of attributing that to the Devil is his inciting people through his whispering (وسوسته) to avoid approaching him, their distancing themselves, and their slandering him—claiming that if he were a Prophet, calamity would not have surrounded him from every side nor would he have reached such an evil end—and their mockery and derision of him.

The author of al-Kashshaf rejected the aforementioned view, saying: "It is not permissible for Allah to give the Devil power over His Prophets (عليه السلام) to fulfill his desire of tormenting and exhausting them. If he were capable of that, he would not have left a righteous person without afflicting and destroying him. It has been repeated in the Quran that he has no authority (سلطان) except for whispering (الوسوسة) alone." End quote.

The response to this is that what is specific to the Prophets and the people of inerrancy (أهل العصمة) is that, by virtue of their inerrancy (عصمتهم), they are secure from the Devil's influence upon their souls through whispering. As for his influence upon their bodies and other things attributed to them by way of harm (إيذاء), exhaustion (إتعاب), or the like, without misguidance (إضلال), there is no evidence indicating its impossibility. Allah the Glorified has narrated from the youth of Moses, who was the Prophet Joshua (يوشع) (عليه السلام): فَإِنِّي نَسِيتُ الْحُوتَ وَما أَنْسانِيهُ إِلَّا الشَّيْطانُ أَنْ أَذْكُرَهُ “I forgot the fish, and none but Satan made me forget to mention it” (18:63).

Furthermore, it does not follow from his being given power over a Prophet for harm and exhaustion—for a wisdom (مصلحة) that requires it, such as the manifestation of his patience (صبره) for Allah the Glorified and his turning (أوبته) to Him—that he is capable of doing whatever he wishes to whomever he wishes among the servants of Allah the Exalted, unless Allah wills that, which is obvious.

His saying, the Exalted: ارْكُضْ بِرِجْلِكَ هذا مُغْتَسَلٌ بارِدٌ وَشَرابٌ “‘Stamp your foot: here is a cool spring for washing and for drink’” (38:42). The occurrence of the verse immediately after his call and request indicates that it is an announcement of the acceptance of his prayer. His saying, the Exalted: "Stamp your foot" (ارْكُضْ بِرِجْلِكَ) etc., is a narration of what was revealed to him upon the disclosure of the acceptance, or it is by assuming a suppressed verb of "saying," the estimation being: "So We answered him and said: Stamp..." etc. The context of the command suggests, or rather reveals, that he was unable to stand or walk on his feet and was afflicted in the rest of his body. So Allah healed the distress in his legs and made a spring appear for him there, commanding him to wash in it and drink so that his body would be healed externally and internally. This is supported by the narration that will follow.

In the speech, there is a conciseness by omission (إيجاز بالحذف), the estimation being: "So he stamped with his foot, washed, and drank, and Allah healed him of his illness."

His saying, the Exalted: وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ أَهْلَهُ وَمِثْلَهُمْ مَعَهُمْ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا وَذِكْرى لِأُولِي الْأَلْبابِ “And We gave him [back] his family, and others like them along with them, as a mercy from Us and an admonition for those who possess intellect” (38:43). It is mentioned in the narration that among his trials was the death of all his family except his wife, and that Allah brought them back to life for him and gave them to him, along with others like them. It is also said that they had dispersed from him during the days of his tribulation, so Allah gathered them back to him after his recovery, and they procreated until they became twice their original number.

His saying: "as a mercy from Us and an admonition for those who possess intellect" (رَحْمَةً مِنَّا وَذِكْرى لِأُولِي الْأَلْبابِ) is an object of purpose (مفعول له), meaning: We did for him what We did so that it might be a mercy from Us and a reminder for those who possess intellect (أولي الألباب) — those who have pure, unadulterated reason — that they may take heed.

His saying, the Exalted: وَخُذْ بِيَدِكَ ضِغْثاً فَاضْرِبْ بِهِ وَلا تَحْنَثْ إِنَّا وَجَدْناهُ صابِراً نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ “‘Take a bunch of rushes in your hand and strike with it, and do not break your oath.’ Indeed, We found him patient. What an excellent servant! Indeed he was a penitent [soul]” (38:44). In al-Majma‘: A bunch (الضغث) is a handful of branches, grass, clusters, or the like. End quote. He (عليه السلام) had sworn that if he were healed, he would lash his wife a hundred times for something he disapproved of her doing, according to what will come in the narration. When Allah healed him, He commanded him to take in his hand a bunch equal to the number of lashes he had sworn, and to strike her with it once, so as not to break his oath (لا يحنث).

The context of the verse alludes to this, though the mention of the woman and the reason for the oath were omitted out of etiquette (تأدباً) and out of regard for his status.

His saying: "Indeed, We found him patient" (إِنَّا وَجَدْناهُ صابِراً) means in what We tested him with of illness and the loss of family and wealth. The sentence is a justification (تعليل) for His saying: "And remember" (وَاذْكُرْ) or for His saying: "Our servant" (عَبْدَنا), i.e., for his being named a servant and his being attributed to the Exalted. The first is more appropriate.

His saying: "What an excellent servant! Indeed he was a penitent [soul]" (نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ) is a praise for him (عليه السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ عِبادَنا إِبْراهِيمَ وَإِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ أُولِي الْأَيْدِي وَالْأَبْصارِ “And remember Our servants, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, men of strength and insight” (38:45). He praised them by describing them as possessing "hands" (الأيدي) and "eyes" (الأبصار). A person's hand and eye are only praised when they are the hand of a human and the eye of a human, used for what they were created for and serving the human in his humanity. Thus, the hand acquires righteous deeds (صالح العمل) and through it, good flows to the creation; and the eye distinguishes the paths of well-being and safety from the places of destruction, hitting upon the truth (الحق) without being confused by falsehood (الباطل).

Therefore, their being "possessors of hands and eyes" is a metaphor (كناية) for their strength in obedience and in delivering good, and their insight (تبصرهم) in attaining the truth in belief and action. He combined the two meanings in His saying, the Exalted: وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نافِلَةً وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنا صالِحِينَ وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْراتِ وَإِقامَ الصَّلاةِ وَإِيتاءَ الزَّكاةِ وَكانُوا لَنا عابِدِينَ “And We gave him Isaac, and Jacob as a well-deserved bounty, and each of them We made righteous. We made them imams, guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat, and they used to worship Us” (21:72-73). He made them Imams, and the "command" and "revelation" pertain to their "eyes" (insight), while the "performance of good deeds," "maintenance of prayer," and "giving of zakat" pertain to their "hands" (strength). To this returns what is in the narration interpreting that as being possessors of strength in worship and insight therein.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا أَخْلَصْناهُمْ بِخالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ “Indeed We purified them with an exclusive quality: the remembrance of the [ultimate] abode” (38:46). The "exclusive quality" (الخالصة) is an adjective standing in place of its noun. The preposition "with" (بـ) denotes causality (السببية), and the estimation is: "because of an exclusive trait." The "remembrance of the abode" (ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ) is an explanation of the trait, and the "abode" (الدار) is the Abode of the Hereafter.

The verse—meaning His saying: "Indeed We purified them" (إِنَّا أَخْلَصْناهُمْ) etc.—is to justify what was in the previous verse: "men of strength and insight" (أُولِي الْأَيْدِي وَالْأَبْصارِ), or for "Our servants" (عِبادَنا), or for "And remember" (وَاذْكُرْ). The most likely of these is the first. This is because a person's immersion in the remembrance of the Abode of the Hereafter and the proximity of the Lord of the worlds, and the centering of his concern therein, necessitates the perfection of his gnosis (معرفته) regarding Allah, the correctness of his vision regarding the truth of belief, and insight in traversing the path of servitude (العبودية), as well as liberation from being frozen upon the outward appearance of the life of this world and its finery, as is the state of the children of this world. The Exalted said: فأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ “So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge” (53:29-30).

The meaning of the verse is: They were only possessors of strength and insight because We purified them with an exclusive trait, unmixed and of great significance, which is the remembrance of the Abode of the Hereafter.

It is also said: The "abode" (الدار) refers to this world, and the meaning of the verse is the persistence of their beautiful mention on the tongues as long as the world lasts, as the Exalted said: وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ — until He said — وَجَعَلْنا لَهُمْ لِسانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا “And We gave him Isaac and Jacob... and We assigned them a high and true renown” (19:49-50). However, the previous view is more likely.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّهُمْ عِنْدَنا لَمِنَ الْمُصْطَفَيْنَ الْأَخْيارِ “Indeed they are with Us among the chosen and the best” (38:47). It has been mentioned previously that being chosen (الاصطفاء) necessitates complete submission (الإسلام التام) to Allah the Glorified. In the verse is an allusion to His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ اصْطَفى آدَمَ وَنُوحاً وَآلَ إِبْراهِيمَ وَآلَ عِمْرانَ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ “Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah, and the progeny of Abraham and the progeny of ‘Imran above all the worlds” (3:33).

The "best" (الأخيار) is the plural of "good" (خير), the opposite of evil, as has been said. It is also said to be the plural of khayyir (خير) with a shaddah or without, like amwat (أموات) is the plural of mayyit (ميت).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ إِسْماعِيلَ وَالْيَسَعَ وَذَا الْكِفْلِ وَكُلٌّ مِنَ الْأَخْيارِ “And remember Ishmael, Elisha and Dhul-Kifl—each [of them] among the best” (38:48). Its meaning is clear.

(A Discussion on the Story of Job (عليه‌السلام) in Several Sections)

1 - His story in the Quran: Nothing of his story is mentioned in the Quran except his being tested with distress (الضر) in his person and his children, then the Exalted's relief of it by healing him and giving him his family and others like them along with them, as a mercy from Him and an admonition for the worshippers (21:83-84; 38:41-44).

2 - His beautiful praise: The Exalted mentioned him among the group of Prophets from the progeny of Abraham (عليه السلام) in Surah al-An‘am and praised them with every beautiful praise (6:84-90). He also mentioned him in Surah Sad, counting him as patient (صابراً), an excellent servant (نعم العبد), and penitent (أواباً) (38:44).

His story in the narrations: In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: My father narrated to me from Ibn Faddal from ‘Abdullah ibn Bahr from Ibn Muskan from Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), who said: I asked him about the tribulation of Job with which he was tested in the world—for what reason was it? He said: "For a blessing that Allah (عز وجل) had bestowed upon him in the world, for which he had rendered thanks. In those days, Iblis was not veiled from beneath the Throne. When he ascended and saw Job's gratitude for the blessing, Iblis envied him.

He said: 'O Lord, Job has only rendered thanks to You for this blessing because of what You have given him of the world. If You were to deprive him of his world, he would never render thanks to You for a blessing. So give me power over his world, so that You may know that he has never rendered thanks to You for a blessing.' It was said to him: 'I have given you power over his wealth and his children.'

He said: So Iblis descended and did not leave him any wealth or child but that he destroyed them. Yet Job increased in gratitude and praise to Allah. He said: 'O Lord, give me power over his crops.' He said: 'I have done so.' So he came with his devils and blew upon them, and they were burned. Yet Job increased in gratitude and praise to Allah. He said: 'O Lord, give me power over his sheep.' So he destroyed them. Yet Job increased in gratitude and praise to Allah.

He said: 'O Lord, give me power over his body.' So He gave him power over his body, except for his intellect and his eyes. Iblis blew upon him, and he became a single sore from his crown to his feet. He remained in that state for a long time, praising Allah and thanking Him, until worms appeared in his body. They would come out of his body, and he would return them, saying to them: 'Return to your place from which Allah created you.' He began to stench until the people of the village expelled him from the village and threw him onto the dung heap outside the village.

His wife was Rahmah, the daughter of Ephraim son of Joseph son of Jacob son of Isaac son of Abraham (عليه‌السلام). She would beg from the people for him and bring him what she could find.

He said: When the trial lasted long for him and Iblis saw his patience, he came to some companions of Job who were monks in the mountains and said to them: 'Let us go to this afflicted servant and ask him about his tribulation.' They rode white mules and came. When they drew near him, their mules bolted from the stench of his odor. They looked at one another, then walked to him. Among them was a young man of tender age. They sat by him and said: 'O Job, if you would tell us of your sin, perhaps Allah would destroy us if we asked Him. We do not see your being tested with this tribulation—which no one has been tested with—except because of a matter you were concealing.'

Job said: 'By the might of my Lord, He knows that I never ate food except that an orphan or a weak person ate with me. And never were two matters presented to me, both of which were obedience to Allah, except that I took the harder of the two upon my body.' The young man said: 'Woe to you! You have reproached the Prophet of Allah until he revealed of the worship of his Lord what he used to conceal.'

Job said: 'O Lord, if I were to sit in the seat of judgment from You, I would present my argument.' So Allah sent a cloud to him and said: 'O Job, present your argument, for I have seated you in the seat of judgment, and here I am near, as I have always been.'

He said: 'O Lord, You know that never were two matters presented to me, both of which were obedience to You, except that I took the harder of the two upon myself. Did I not praise You? Did I not thank You? Did I not glorify You?'

He said: Then he was called from the cloud with ten thousand tongues: 'O Job, who made you worship Allah while the people were heedless of Him? And praise Him, glorify Him, and magnify Him while the people were heedless of Him? Do you claim a favor upon Allah for that in which the favor of Allah is upon you?' He said: So he took dust and put it in his mouth, then said: 'To You belongs the right to reproach, O Lord; You did that to me.'

So Allah sent an angel to him who stamped with his foot, and water came out. He washed him with that water, and he returned to being better and fresher than he had been. Allah made a green meadow grow over him, and returned to him his family, wealth, children, and crops. The angel sat with him, talking to him and keeping him company.

His wife approached with a piece of bread. When she reached the place, the place had changed, and there were two men sitting. She wept and cried out, saying: 'O Job, what has happened to you?' Job called her, and she approached. When she saw him and that Allah had restored his body and his blessings, she prostrated to Allah in gratitude. He saw that her braid was cut. This was because she had asked some people to give her food to carry to Job—and she had beautiful braids—and they said to her: 'Will you sell us this braid of yours so we may give to you?' So she cut it and gave it to them and took food for Job. When he saw her hair cut, he became angry and swore to strike her a hundred times. She informed him that the reason was such and such. Job became grieved by that, so Allah (عز وجل) revealed to him: خُذْ بِيَدِكَ ضِغْثاً فَاضْرِبْ بِهِ وَلا تَحْنَثْ 'Take a bunch of rushes in your hand and strike with it, and do not break your oath.' So he took a branch containing a hundred twigs and struck her a single strike, and thus he fulfilled his oath."

I say: Something similar is narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas. From Wahb, it is narrated that his wife was the daughter of Misha son of Joseph. The narration—as you see—mentions his being tested with things that natural dispositions find repulsive, and there are narrations that support that. However, some reports narrated from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه‌السلام) negate that and reject it most severely, as follows:

From al-Khisal: al-Qattan from al-Sukkari from al-Jawhari from Ibn ‘Amarah from his father from Ja‘far ibn Muhammad from his father (عليه‌السلام), who said: "Job (عليه‌السلام) was tested for seven years without a sin—and indeed the Prophets do not sin because they are inerrant (معصومون) and purified (مطهرون); they do not sin, nor do they deviate, nor do they commit a sin, small or great."

He said: "Job, in all that he was tested with, never had a foul odor, nor did his form become ugly, nor did any discharge of blood or pus come from him, nor did anyone who saw him find him loathsome, nor was anyone who witnessed him repelled by him, nor did any part of his body become worm-infested. This is how Allah (عز وجل) acts with all those He tests among His Prophets and His honored friends (أوليائه).

People only avoided him because of his poverty and weakness in his outward affair, due to their ignorance of the support and relief he had with his Lord, may His mention be exalted. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'The people most severely tested are the Prophets, then the next best, then the next best.'

Allah only tested him with the great tribulation—which makes him seem insignificant to all people—so that they would not claim divinity (الربوبية) for him when they witnessed the great blessings Allah intended to bring to him; and so they would deduce from that that reward from Allah is of two types: entitlement (استحقاق) and special selection (اختصاص); and so they would not despise a weak person for his weakness, nor a poor person for his poverty, nor a sick person for his illness; and so they would know that He makes ill whom He wills and heals whom He wills, whenever He wills, however He wills, by whatever cause He wills, making that a lesson for whom He wills, a misery for whom He wills, and a happiness for whom He wills. He (عز وجل) in all of that is just in His decree and wise in His acts; He does not do with His servants except what is most beneficial for them, and they have no power except through Him."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ أَهْلَهُ وَمِثْلَهُمْ مَعَهُمْ etc., he said: "Allah returned to him his family who had died before the tribulation, and He returned to him his family who had died after the tribulation befell them; Allah brought them all back to life for him, and they lived with him."

Job was asked after Allah healed him: "What thing was hardest upon you of what passed?" He said: "The mockery of the enemies (شماتة الأعداء)."

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَنِّي مَسَّنِيَ الشَّيْطانُ etc., it is said: "His illness became so severe that people avoided him. Then the Devil whispered to the people to find him loathsome and to expel him from among them, and not to let his wife who served him enter upon them. Job was pained by that and suffered from it, but he did not complain of the pain that was from the command of Allah the Glorified." Qatadah said: "That lasted for seven years." This is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام).

(The Report of Elisha and Dhul-Kifl (عليه‌السلام))

The Glorified mentioned their names in His speech, counted them among the Prophets, praised them, and counted them among the best (الأخيار) (38:48). He also counted Dhul-Kifl among the patient (الصابرين) (21:85). They are mentioned in the reports.

In al-Bihar, from al-Ihtijaj, al-Tawhid, and al-‘Uyun, in a long report narrated by al-Hasan ibn Muhammad al-Nawfali from Imam al-Rida (عليه‌السلام) in what he argued against the Catholicos of the Christians, he (عليه‌السلام) said: "Elisha (اليسع) performed the like of what Jesus (عليه‌السلام) performed—he walked on water, brought the dead to life, and healed the blind and the leper—yet his nation did not take him as a lord." The report continues.

From Qisas al-Anbiya’: al-Saduq from al-Daqqaq from al-Asadi from Sahl from ‘Abd al-‘Azim al-Hasani, who said: I wrote to Abu Ja‘far the Second (Imam al-Jawad) asking him about Dhul-Kifl: what was his name? And was he among the messengers (المرسلين)?

He (عليه‌السلام) wrote: "Allah, may His mention be exalted, sent one hundred and twenty-four thousand Prophets. Among them were three hundred and thirteen messengers, and Dhul-Kifl was among them. He was after Solomon son of David. He used to judge between the people as David used to judge, and he never became angry except for the sake of Allah (عز وجل). His name was ‘Uwaydiya—and he is the one whom Allah, may His greatness be exalted, mentioned in His Book where He said: وَاذْكُرْ إِسْماعِيلَ وَالْيَسَعَ وَذَا الْكِفْلِ وَكُلٌّ مِنَ الْأَخْيارِ 'And remember Ishmael, Elisha and Dhul-Kifl—each [of them] among the best.'"

I say: There are other scattered narrations regarding their stories (عليه السلام) which we have omitted due to their weakness and lack of reliability.

Ayahs

هَـٰذَا ذِكْرٌ ۚ وَإِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ لَحُسْنَ مَـَٔابٍ (٤٩)
This is a Reminder, and indeed the Godwary have a good destination: (49)
جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ مُّفَتَّحَةً لَّهُمُ ٱلْأَبْوَٰبُ (٥٠)
the Gardens of Eden, whose gates will be flung open for them. (50)
مُتَّكِـِٔينَ فِيهَا يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا بِفَـٰكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ وَشَرَابٍ (٥١)
Reclining therein [on couches], they will call for abundant fruits and drinks, (51)
۞ وَعِندَهُمْ قَـٰصِرَٰتُ ٱلطَّرْفِ أَتْرَابٌ (٥٢)
and there will be with them maidens of restrained glances, of a like age. (52)
هَـٰذَا مَا تُوعَدُونَ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْحِسَابِ (٥٣)
This is what you are promised on the Day of Reckoning. (53)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَرِزْقُنَا مَا لَهُۥ مِن نَّفَادٍ (٥٤)
This is Our provision, which will never be exhausted. (54)
هَـٰذَا ۚ وَإِنَّ لِلطَّـٰغِينَ لَشَرَّ مَـَٔابٍ (٥٥)
This [will be for the righteous], and as for the rebellious there will surely be a bad destination: (55)
جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَهَا فَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ (٥٦)
hell, which they shall enter, an evil resting place. (56)
هَـٰذَا فَلْيَذُوقُوهُ حَمِيمٌ وَغَسَّاقٌ (٥٧)
[They will be told, ‘This is scalding water and pus; let them taste it, (57)
وَءَاخَرُ مِن شَكْلِهِۦٓ أَزْوَٰجٌ (٥٨)
and other kinds [of torments] resembling it.’ (58)
هَـٰذَا فَوْجٌ مُّقْتَحِمٌ مَّعَكُمْ ۖ لَا مَرْحَبًۢا بِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ صَالُوا۟ ٱلنَّارِ (٥٩)
[The leaders of the faithless will be told,] ‘This is a group [of your followers] plunging [into hell] along with you.’ [They will repond,] ‘May wretchedness be their lot! For they will enter the Fire.’ (59)
قَالُوا۟ بَلْ أَنتُمْ لَا مَرْحَبًۢا بِكُمْ ۖ أَنتُمْ قَدَّمْتُمُوهُ لَنَا ۖ فَبِئْسَ ٱلْقَرَارُ (٦٠)
They will say, ‘No, may wretchedness be your lot! You prepared this [hell] for us. What an evil abode!’ (60)
قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا مَن قَدَّمَ لَنَا هَـٰذَا فَزِدْهُ عَذَابًا ضِعْفًا فِى ٱلنَّارِ (٦١)
They will say, ‘Our Lord! Whoever has prepared this for us, double his punishment in the Fire!’ (61)
وَقَالُوا۟ مَا لَنَا لَا نَرَىٰ رِجَالًا كُنَّا نَعُدُّهُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَشْرَارِ (٦٢)
And they will say, ‘Why is it that we do not see [here] men whom we used to count among the bad ones, (62)
أَتَّخَذْنَـٰهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا أَمْ زَاغَتْ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ (٦٣)
ridiculing them, or do [our] eyes miss them [here]?’ (63)
إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ لَحَقٌّ تَخَاصُمُ أَهْلِ ٱلنَّارِ (٦٤)
That is indeed a true account of the contentions of the inmates of the Fire. (64)

(Commentary)

This is another section of the discourse wherein the final destination of the God-conscious (المتقين) and the rebellious (الطاغين) is explained by way of glad tidings and warning.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا ذِكْرٌ وَإِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ لَحُسْنَ مَآبٍ "This is a reminder; and indeed for the God-conscious there is a fair resort" (38:49). The word "this" (هذا) refers to the aforementioned accounts of the "oft-returning" (الأوابين) noble Prophets (peace be upon them). The intended meaning of "reminder" (الذكر) is honor and beautiful praise; that is, what has been mentioned is an honor, a beautiful remembrance, and a fair praise for them, by which they shall be remembered in the world forever, and for them is a fair resort (حسن مآب) consisting of the reward of the Hereafter. This is what the commentators have said.

Based on this, the "God-conscious" (المتقين) refers specifically to the mentioned Prophets, or to the generality of the people of godwariness (أهل التقوى), with the Prophets being included among them. In this case, the subsequent mention of the resort of the rebellious would be by way of digression (استطراد).

However, it appears more likely that "this" (هذا) refers to the Quran, and "reminder" (الذكر) refers to the remembrance it contains. Thus, the discourse returns to what the Surah began with in His saying: وَالْقُرْآنِ ذِي الذِّكْرِ "By the Quran, possessor of the reminder" (38:1). It is a section of the speech in which Allah, the Glorified, mentions the reward of the God-conscious and the punishment of the rebellious in the abode of the Hereafter.

His saying: وَإِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ لَحُسْنَ مَآبٍ "and indeed for the God-conscious there is a fair resort"—the resort (المآب) is the place of return (المرجع), and the use of the indefinite form (التنكير) is for magnification (تفخيم). The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ مُفَتَّحَةً لَهُمُ الْأَبْوابُ "the Gardens of Eden, with their gates opened for them" (38:50). That is, gardens of stability and eternity (خلود). The gates being "opened for them" is a metaphor (كناية) for the fact that they are not barred from any of the blessings present therein; the gardens are prepared for them and created for their sake. It has also been said that it means the gates are open for them such that they do not need to stand behind them or knock. Others have said it means they open without a key and close without a bolt.

This verse and those following it are an exposition (بيان) of their "fair resort."

His saying, the Exalted: مُتَّكِئِينَ فِيها يَدْعُونَ فِيها بِفاكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ وَشرابٍ "Reclining therein, they will call therein for abundant fruits and drinks" (38:51). That is, while they are seated therein in a state of reclining and leaning back—the seating posture of the mighty and the noble.

His saying: يَدْعُونَ فِيها بِفاكِهَةٍ "they will call therein for fruits," etc., means they will command whatever they desire by calling for fruit—which is abundant—and drink. When a fruit or drink is called for, the thing called responds to them and comes to them without the need for someone to carry or hand it to them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَعِنْدَهُمْ قاصِراتُ الطَّرْفِ أَتْرابٌ "And with them will be maidens of restrained glances, of an equal age" (38:52). The pronoun refers to the God-conscious. "Maidens of restrained glances" (قاصرات الطرف) is an attribute standing in place of the noun it describes; the implied meaning is "and with them are spouses of restrained glances." This refers to their glances being restricted to their own spouses; they are well-pleased with them and look at no one else. Alternatively, it is a metaphor (كناية) for their being possessors of coquetry and grace (غنج ودلال).

The "equal in age" (الأتراب) are peers (الأقران); meaning they are alike, not differing in age or beauty. Or it means they are matches for their spouses: whenever the spouses increase in light and splendor, the maidens increase in excellence and beauty.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا ما تُوعَدُونَ لِيَوْمِ الْحِسابِ "This is what you are promised for the Day of Reckoning" (38:53). The word "this" refers to the mentioned Paradise and its bliss. The address is to the God-conscious; thus, there is a transition (التفات) in the speech from the third person to the second person. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to demonstrate proximity to them and oversight over them, so that their formal blessings may be completed by this spiritual blessing.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هذا لَرِزْقُنا ما لَهُ مِنْ نَفادٍ "Indeed this is Our provision, which will never be exhausted" (38:54). Exhaustion (النفاد) is cessation and termination. The verse is a continuation of the address in the previous verse, as indicated by the context.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا وَإِنَّ لِلطَّاغِينَ لَشَرَّ مَآبٍ "So [will it be]; but indeed for the rebellious there will be an evil resort" (38:55). The word "this" (هذا) refers to the aforementioned station of the God-conscious; i.e., "this is the resort for the God-conscious." It is also possible that "this" is a verbal noun (اسم فعل) meaning "take this." The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَها فَبِئْسَ الْمِهادُ "Hell, which they shall enter—an evil resting place!" (38:56). Entering (الصلي) means entering the Fire and enduring its heat, or being followed by it. The "resting place" (المهاد)—according to al-Majma‘—is a spread-out bed (الفراش الموطأ). It is said: "I prepared (مهدت) for him a preparation," similar to "I smoothed (وطأت) for him a smoothing." This verse and those following it are an interpretation (تفسير) of the resort of the rebellious.

His saying, the Exalted: هذا فَلْيَذُوقُوهُ حَمِيمٌ وَغَسَّاقٌ "This!—let them taste it—scalding water and pus" (38:57). The "scalding water" (الحميم) is water of intense heat. The "pus" (الغساق)—according to al-Majma‘—is discharge of extreme stench; it has also been interpreted in other ways. His saying: حَمِيمٌ وَغَسَّاقٌ is an exposition (بيان) of "this." His saying: فَلْيَذُوقُوهُ "let them taste it" indicates their being coerced and forced to taste it. Placing the subject first and making it a demonstrative pronoun emphasizes this. The meaning is: "This is scalding water and pus; they must taste it, and nothing else."

His saying, the Exalted: وَآخَرُ مِنْ شَكْلِهِ أَزْواجٌ "and others of a similar kind, in pairs" (38:58). The "likeness" (شكل) of a thing is what resembles it and is of its genus. "Pairs" (الأزواج) refers to types and categories. That is: "and this is another of the genus of scalding water and pus, of various types for them to taste."

His saying, the Exalted: هذا فَوْجٌ مُقْتَحِمٌ مَعَكُمْ "‘This is a company plunging in with you...’" (38:59) until His saying: فِي النَّارِ "...in the Fire" (38:61). These three verses—as indicated by the context—are a narration of what occurs between the followers (التابعين) and the followed (المتبوعين) among the rebellious in the Fire, in terms of disputation and contention.

His saying: هذا فَوْجٌ مُقْتَحِمٌ مَعَكُمْ "This is a company plunging in with you" is an address directed to the leaders (المتبوعون), referring to the followers who enter the Fire with the leaders as a "company" (فوج). "Plunging" (الاقتحام) is to enter into something with force and difficulty.

His saying: لا مَرْحَباً بِهِمْ إِنَّهُمْ صالُوا النَّارِ "‘No welcome to them! They shall indeed enter the Fire’" (38:59) is the response of the leaders to the one addressing them with "This is a company..." The term "welcome" (مرحباً) is a greeting for the one arriving, meaning "may you find the abode spacious (رحب) and wide." Thus, their saying "No welcome to them" means a denial of spaciousness and ease for them. Their saying "They shall indeed enter the Fire" (إِنَّهُمْ صالُوا النَّارِ)—meaning they are entering it and enduring its heat, or following into it—is the justification (تعليل) for greeting them with the denial of a greeting.

His saying: قالُوا بَلْ أَنْتُمْ لا مَرْحَباً بِكُمْ أَنْتُمْ قَدَّمْتُمُوهُ لَنا فَبِئْسَ الْقَرارُ "They will say, ‘No, it is you! No welcome to you! It was you who brought it upon us. What an evil settling place!’" (38:60) relates the speech of the followers. They are the ones speaking, returning the denial of greeting to their leaders and disparaging the "settling place" (القرار) in the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا رَبَّنا مَنْ قَدَّمَ لَنا هذا فَزِدْهُ عَذاباً ضِعْفاً فِي النَّارِ "They will say, ‘Our Lord! Whoever brought this upon us, double his punishment in the Fire’" (38:61). The Exalted did not mention the response of the leaders to their saying "It was you who brought it upon us," though He mentioned it in Surah al-Saffat in what He narrated of their questioning: قالُوا بَلْ لَمْ تَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ وَما كانَ لَنا عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ بَلْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً طاغِينَ "They will say, ‘No, you [yourselves] were not faithful. We had no authority over you; rather you were a rebellious people’" (37:29-30). Thus, their saying "Our Lord! Whoever brought this upon us..." is their speech after the cessation of the disputation.

The sentence "Whoever brought..." (مَنْ قَدَّمَ) is a conditional and its result. "Double" (الضعف) means the like of it; "a double punishment" (عَذاباً ضِعْفاً) means one possessing a double, i.e., two-fold punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا ما لَنا لا نَرى رِجالاً كُنَّا نَعُدُّهُمْ مِنَ الْأَشْرارِ "And they will say, ‘What is the matter with us that we do not see the men whom we used to count among the wicked?’" (38:62). The speakers—as the context suggests—are the people of the Fire in general. By "the men whom we used to count among the wicked," they mean the believers, who are in Paradise. The people of the Fire search for them but do not find them there.

His saying, the Exalted: أَتَّخَذْناهُمْ سِخْرِيًّا أَمْ زاغَتْ عَنْهُمُ الْأَبْصارُ "‘Did we [mistakenly] take them for a laughingstock, or have [our] eyes missed them?’" (38:63). That is: Did we take them as a laughingstock in the world and were thus mistaken, while they were actually saved? Or have our eyes turned away from them such that we do not see them while they are actually with us in the Fire?

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ ذلِكَ لَحَقٌّ تَخاصُمُ أَهْلِ النَّارِ "That is indeed true: the disputing of the people of the Fire" (38:64). This refers to the narrated disputation and is an explanation that the disputing of the people of the Fire is a fixed reality (حق) in which there is no doubt. It is the manifestation of the habit (ملكة) of contention and quarreling that was established in their souls during the life of the world.

Ayahs

قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ مُنذِرٌ ۖ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ (٦٥)
Say, ‘I am just a warner, and there is no god except Allah, the One, the All-paramount, (65)
رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْغَفَّـٰرُ (٦٦)
the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, the All-mighty, the All-forgiver.’ (66)
قُلْ هُوَ نَبَؤٌا۟ عَظِيمٌ (٦٧)
Say, ‘It is a great prophesy, (67)
أَنتُمْ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضُونَ (٦٨)
of which you are disregardful. (68)
مَا كَانَ لِىَ مِنْ عِلْمٍۭ بِٱلْمَلَإِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰٓ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ (٦٩)
I have no knowledge of the Supernal Elite when they contend. (69)
إِن يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ إِلَّآ أَنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧٠)
All that is revealed to me is that I am just a manifest warner.’ (70)
إِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ إِنِّى خَـٰلِقٌۢ بَشَرًا مِّن طِينٍ (٧١)
When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am about to create a human being out of clay. (71)
فَإِذَا سَوَّيْتُهُۥ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِن رُّوحِى فَقَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ سَـٰجِدِينَ (٧٢)
So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down in prostration before him.’ (72)
فَسَجَدَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ (٧٣)
Thereat the angels prostrated, all of them together, (73)
إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ ٱسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٧٤)
but not Iblis; he acted arrogantly and he was one of the faithless. (74)
قَالَ يَـٰٓإِبْلِيسُ مَا مَنَعَكَ أَن تَسْجُدَ لِمَا خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَىَّ ۖ أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْعَالِينَ (٧٥)
He said, ‘O Iblis! What keeps you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands? Are you arrogant, or are you one of the exalted ones?’ (75)
قَالَ أَنَا۠ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهُ ۖ خَلَقْتَنِى مِن نَّارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُۥ مِن طِينٍ (٧٦)
‘I am better than him,’ he said. ‘You created me from fire and You created him from clay.’ (76)
قَالَ فَٱخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ (٧٧)
He said, ‘Begone hence, for you are indeed an outcast, (77)
وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ (٧٨)
and indeed My curse will be on you till the Day of Retribution.’ (78)
قَالَ رَبِّ فَأَنظِرْنِىٓ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (٧٩)
He said, ‘My Lord! Respite me till the day they will be resurrected.’ (79)
قَالَ فَإِنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنظَرِينَ (٨٠)
Said He, ‘You are indeed among the reprieved (80)
إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْوَقْتِ ٱلْمَعْلُومِ (٨١)
until the day of the known time.’ (81)
قَالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٨٢)
He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them, (82)
إِلَّا عِبَادَكَ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْمُخْلَصِينَ (٨٣)
except Your exclusive servants among them.’ (83)
قَالَ فَٱلْحَقُّ وَٱلْحَقَّ أَقُولُ (٨٤)
Said He, ‘The truth is that—and I speak the truth— (84)
لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنكَ وَمِمَّن تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٨٥)
I will surely fill hell with you and all those who follow you.’ (85)
قُلْ مَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ (٨٦)
Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, and I am no impostor. (86)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٨٧)
It is just a reminder for all the nations, (87)
وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ نَبَأَهُۥ بَعْدَ حِينٍۭ (٨٨)
and you will surely learn its tidings in due time.’ (88)

(Commentary)

This is the final section of the Surah’s chapters, comprising the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to convey his warning and his call to monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts. It establishes that turning away from the truth and following Satan leads man to the punishment of the Fire, which has been decreed against him and his followers. At this point, the Surah concludes.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا مُنْذِرٌ وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ (Say, ‘I am only a warner, and there is no god except Allah, the One, the All-paramount) until His saying الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفَّارُ (the All-mighty, the All-forgiver). In these two verses, the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is commanded to proclaim that he is a warner and that Allah, the Exalted, is One in His divinity. His saying, "I am only a warner" (إِنَّما أَنَا مُنْذِرٌ), serves to restrict his role to that of warning and to negate all other motives that people might associate with such a call, such as seeking wealth or status. This is further indicated at the end of the verses by his saying: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ (Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, nor am I one of those who fabricate’).

His saying: وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ (and there is no god except Allah) until the end of the two verses is a proclamation of His monotheism (توحيده) through an argument derived from the attributes signified by His names.

His saying: وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ is a negation of every deity (إله)—and the deity is the one truly worthy of worship (المعبود بالحق)—other than Him, the Exalted. As for the affirmation of His divinity, it is accepted as a given once the divinity of others is negated, for there is no dispute between Islam and polytheism (الشرك) regarding the fundamental existence of a Deity; rather, the dispute lies in whether the Deity—the one truly worthy of worship—is Allah, the Exalted, or someone else. Furthermore, the attributes mentioned in the two verses contain the affirmation of His divinity just as they serve as a proof for the negation of the divinity of any other than Him.

His saying: الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ (the One, the All-paramount) indicates His uniqueness in His existence and His subjugation of all things. This is because He, the Exalted, is One (واحد); nothing resembles Him in His existence, and there is no limit to His perfection, which is identical to His Necessary Existence (وجوده الواجب). Thus, He is Self-Sufficient (الغني بذاته) in an absolute sense, while everything else is poor (فقير), needing Him from every aspect. Nothing possesses existence or the effects of existence except what He has bestowed and emanated. Therefore, He, the Glorified, is the Subduer (القاهر) of everything according to His will, and everything is obedient to Him in what He intends, humbled before Him in what He desires.

This essential humility (الخضوع الذاتي) is the reality of worship (العبادة). If it were permissible for anything in existence to be worshipped—by performing an act that represents servitude and humility—it would be the worship of Him, the Glorified. For everything assumed besides Him is subjugated and humbled before Him, possessing nothing for itself or for others, and having no independence in existence or the effects of existence. Thus, He, the Glorified, is the Deity truly worthy of worship, and none other.

His saying: رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُمَا (the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them) provides another proof for His uniqueness in divinity. This is because the system of governance (نظام التدبير) operating in the entire world is a single, connected system, neither partitioned nor fragmented. This is a sign of the unity of the Governor (المدبر). It has been mentioned repeatedly that creation (الخلق) and governance (التدبير) are inseparable.

Governance is creation from one perspective, just as creation is governance from another. The Creator who brought the heavens, the earth, and what is between them into existence is Allah, the Glorified—even according to the opponent. Therefore, He, the Exalted, is their Lord and their Governor. Consequently, He alone is the Deity who must be sought through worship, because worship is the representation of the worshipper's servitude and state of being owned (مملوكيته) in the face of the Mastership (مولوية) and Ownership (مالكية) of the Worshipped, and His disposal over the worshipper by bestowing blessings and averting calamities. Thus, He, the Glorified, is the Deity in the heavens, the earth, and what is between them; there is no god but He. Understand this well.

It is possible that His saying: رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما بَيْنَهُمَا serves as an explanation for His saying "the All-paramount" (الْقَهَّارُ) or "the One, the All-paramount" (الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ).

His saying: الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفَّارُ (the All-mighty, the All-forgiver) provides yet another proof for His uniqueness in divinity. This is because He, the Exalted, is Mighty (عزيز); nothing can overcome Him by forcing Him into what He does not intend or preventing Him from what He intends. He is the Mighty in an absolute sense, and everything else is lowly (ذليل) before Him, devoutly obedient (قانت) to Him. Worship is the manifestation of lowliness, and it is only valid when directed toward Might (العزة), and there is no might for any other than Him except through Him.

Furthermore, the ultimate goal of worship—which is the representation of servitude—is seeking proximity to the Worshipped and removing the blemish of distance from the worshipping servant, which is the forgiveness of sin. Allah, the Glorified, is the sole possessor of the mercy whose treasures are never exhausted, and He is the one who admits His worshipping servants into the Abode of His Honor in the Hereafter. Thus, He is the All-Forgiver (الغفار) who must be worshipped out of hope for His forgiveness.

It is also possible that His saying: الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفَّارُ is an allusion to the reason for the call to monotheism or the necessity of faith in Him, understood from the context of His saying: وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ. The meaning would be: I call you to His oneness, so believe in Him, for He is the Mighty who is untainted by any lowliness, the Forgiver of sins; and such must the Deity be.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ هُوَ نَبَأٌ عَظِيمٌ أَنْتُمْ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضُونَ (Say, ‘It is a great prophecy, from which you are turning away) The pronoun "It" (هو) refers to the discourse on divine unity mentioned in His saying: وَما مِنْ إِلهٍ إِلَّا اللهُ etc.

It has been said that the pronoun refers to the Quran; thus, it is the "great prophecy" from which they turned away. This is more consistent with the context of the preceding verses related to the matter of the Quran, and also more consistent with His subsequent saying: ما كانَ لِي مِنْ عِلْمٍ بِالْمَلَإِ الْأَعْلى إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ (I had no knowledge of the Highest Assembly when they were contending), meaning: until the Quran informed me of it. It has also been said that it refers to the Day of Resurrection, but this is the most remote of the possibilities.

His saying, the Exalted: ما كانَ لِي مِنْ عِلْمٍ بِالْمَلَإِ الْأَعْلى إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ (I had no knowledge of the Highest Assembly when they were contending). The Highest Assembly (الملأ الأعلى) refers to the company of angels. It seems the "contending" (اختصامهم) refers to what Allah alluded to in His saying: إِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً (When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth’) until the end of the verses.

The meaning seems to be: I did not know of the contention of the Highest Assembly until Allah revealed it to me in His Book; for I am only a warner who follows the revelation (الوحي).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ إِلَّا أَنَّما أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ (Nothing is revealed to me except that I am a manifest warner). This is an emphasis of His saying: "I am only a warner" (إِنَّما أَنَا مُنْذِرٌ), and it serves as a justification for His saying: "I had no knowledge of the Highest Assembly." The meaning is: I did not know that because my knowledge does not come from myself; rather, it is through revelation, and nothing is revealed to me except what pertains to warning.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً مِنْ طِينٍ (When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am about to create a human out of clay’). What the context suggests is that this verse and those following it are not a continuation of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) speech: "I am only a warner," etc. The evidence for this is His saying: "your Lord" (رَبُّكَ). Thus, it is from the speech of Allah, the Exalted, referring to the time of the contention of the Highest Assembly. The adverbial phrase of time (الظرف) is connected to what His saying "when they were contending" (إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ) is connected to, or it is connected to an omitted verb, the estimation being: "Mention when your Lord said to the angels," etc. For Allah’s saying to the angels: "Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth" and His saying to them: "Indeed I am about to create a human out of clay" were concurrent, occurring at the same time.

Based on this, the meaning of His saying: "When your Lord said" (إِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ), etc., resolves to something like our saying: Mention at that time when your Lord said such and such; for that was the time of their contention.

Some have considered His saying: "When your Lord said," etc., as an explanation for His saying: "when they were contending" (إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ). They then took the "contending"—after interpreting it as an exchange of words—to encompass the entirety of Allah’s saying to the angels: "Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth," their saying: "Will You set therein...?" etc., His speech to Adam and Adam’s speech to them, His saying to them: "Indeed I am about to create a human," the speech of Iblis, and Allah’s speech to him.

It was said that if "contending" is taken to mean a dispute, and the phrase "when they were contending" indicates that the dispute was among the angels themselves—not between them and Allah, the Glorified—then Allah’s informing them by His saying: "Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth" and "Indeed I am about to create a human" was through the mediation of an angel. Likewise, His speech to Adam and Iblis. Thus, their saying to their Lord: "Will You set therein one who will cause corruption therein?" etc., would be their speech to the mediating angel, and the contention would occur among themselves.

However, as you know, none of what he mentioned can be inferred from the context of the verses.

His saying: إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً مِنْ طِينٍ (Indeed I am about to create a human out of clay). Human (البشر) refers to the person (الإنسان). Al-Raghib said: al-bashar is the outer skin, while al-adamah is the inner layer. This is what the general grammarians have said. He said: The human is referred to as bashar in consideration of the appearance of his skin without hair, unlike animals that have wool or fur. The word bashar is used for singular, plural, and dual; He, the Exalted, said: أَنُؤْمِنُ لِبَشَرَيْنِ (Shall we believe two humans?). In the Quran, every instance where the human is considered in terms of his physical body and outward appearance is specified with the word bashar. [End of quote].

The verse identifies the starting point of human creation as clay (الطين). In Surah al-Rum, it is dust (التراب); in Surah al-Hijr, it is a dry clay (صلصال) from a molded mud (حمإ مسنون); and in Surah al-Rahman, it is a dry clay like pottery (صلصال كالفخار). There is no contradiction, for these are different states of the original matter from which he was created, and in each instance, one of them is pointed out.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ ساجِدِينَ (So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down before him in prostration). Proportioning (تسوية) the human is the balancing of his limbs by arranging them one upon another and completing them into the form of a perfect human. Breathing the spirit into him (نفخ الروح فيه) is making him possess a living human soul (نفس). The attribution of the spirit to Him, the Exalted, is an attribution of honor (تشريفية). His saying: "then fall down" (فَقَعُوا) is a command derived from "falling," and it is contingent upon the proportioning and the breathing.

His saying, the Exalted: فَسَجَدَ الْمَلائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ (So the angels prostrated, all of them together). This clearly indicates the prostration of the angels to him without exception.

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ اسْتَكْبَرَ وَكانَ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ (except Iblis; he acted arrogantly and was one of the faithless). That is, Iblis acted arrogantly and did not prostrate to him, and he was before that among the disbelievers, as He, the Glorified, narrated from him in Surah al-Hijr: لَمْ أَكُنْ لِأَسْجُدَ لِبَشَرٍ خَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ صَلْصالٍ مِنْ حَمَإٍ مَسْنُونٍ (I am not one to prostrate to a human whom You have created out of a dry clay [drawn] from a molded mud) [al-Hijr: 33].

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا إِبْلِيسُ ما مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِما خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَّ أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْعالِينَ (He said, ‘O Iblis! What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands? Were you arrogant, or were you [one] of the exalted?’). The attribution of his creation to the "hand" is for honor through special distinction, just as He said: "and breathed into him of My spirit." The use of the dual "two hands" (بِيَدَيَّ) is a metaphor for the complete attention given to his creation and fashioning, for a human only uses both hands for work that he deems important. Thus, His saying: "I created with My two hands" is like His saying: مِمَّا عَمِلَتْ أَيْدِينا (of what Our hands have wrought) [Ya-Sin: 71].

It has been said that "hand" means power (القدرة), and the dual is merely for emphasis, like His saying: ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ (Then turn your vision again) [al-Mulk: 3], and a narration has been reported to this effect.

It has also been said that "two hands" refers to the blessings of this world and the Hereafter. It is also possible to interpret the "two hands" as the two origins of body and spirit, or form and meaning, or the two attributes of Majesty (الجلال) and Beauty (الجمال); however, these are meanings for which there is no evidence from the literal wording.

His saying: أَسْتَكْبَرْتَ أَمْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْعالِينَ is a reproachful interrogation. That is: Was your failure to prostrate because you acted arrogantly [now], or were you among those who are exalted (العالين)—meaning those whose rank is too high to be commanded to prostrate? For this reason, some have inferred from the verse that the "exalted" are a group of His creation who are so absorbed in their orientation toward their Lord that they are unaware of anything other than Him, the Exalted.

It has been said that "exaltation" (العلو) means arrogance, as in His saying: وَإِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ لَعالٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ (and indeed Pharaoh was a tyrant in the land) [Yunus: 83]. The meaning would then be: Did you act arrogantly when you were commanded to prostrate, or were you previously among the arrogant?

This is refuted by the fact that it does not suit the requirements of the context; for the context requires the objective of ascertaining the root of his arrogance, not determining whether his arrogance was old or new.

It has also been said that the "exalted" refers to the angels of heaven, for those commanded to prostrate were the angels of the earth. This is refuted by the generality found in the verse.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْهُ خَلَقْتَنِي مِنْ نارٍ وَخَلَقْتَهُ مِنْ طِينٍ (He said, ‘I am better than him: You created me from fire and You created him from clay’). This is a justification for his failure to prostrate based on his claim of the nobility of his essence—that because his creation was from fire, he is better than Adam, who was created from clay. In this is an allusion that the divine command is only to be obeyed if it is "right" (حق) and not for its own sake, and that His command to prostrate to him was not right. This resolves to a denial of the absolute Sovereignty and Wisdom of Him, the Exalted, which is the root from which every sin originates. For sin only occurs by exiting the rule of His servitude and ownership, and by turning away from the fact that leaving the sin is better than committing it.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ فَاخْرُجْ مِنْها فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي إِلى يَوْمِ الدِّينِ (He said, ‘Then get out of it, for you are indeed an outcast, and My curse shall lie on you until the Day of Retribution’). Outcast (الرجم) means being driven away, and the Day of Retribution (يوم الدين) is the Day of Recompense.

Regarding His saying: وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ لَعْنَتِي (and My curse shall lie on you), while in Surah al-Hijr it is: وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكَ اللَّعْنَةَ (and the curse shall lie on you) [verse 35]. It has been said regarding this: If the definite article (اللام) is for specific reference (للعهد), then there is no difference between the two expressions. If it is for the genus (للجنس), it is likewise the same, because the cursing of others—from the angels and humans—upon him is only a true expulsion and distancing from mercy if it is by the command of Allah and His distancing him from His mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ رَبِّ فَأَنْظِرْنِي إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ (He said, ‘My Lord! Respite me until the day they are resurrected’) until His saying إِلى يَوْمِ الْوَقْتِ الْمَعْلُومِ (until the day of the known time). The apparent change in the limit (الغاية) between the request and the answer—where he said: "until the day they are resurrected" and was answered with: "until the day of the known time"—indicates that what was granted is different from what he requested. It is inevitably the last day in which people disobey their Lord, which is before the Day of Resurrection. It appears that "Day" (اليوم) refers to the time-period (الظرف), so its addition to "Time" (الوقت) serves as emphasis.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ إِلَّا عِبادَكَ مِنْهُمُ الْمُخْلَصِينَ (He said, ‘By Your might, I will surely pervert them all, except Your devoted servants among them’). The particle ba in فَبِعِزَّتِكَ is for an oath; he swore by His might that he would surely pervert them all, and he excepted the devoted ones (المخلصين)—those whom Allah has purified for Himself, such that neither Iblis nor anyone else has any share in them.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ فَالْحَقُّ وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (He said, ‘This is the truth, and the truth I speak: I will surely fill hell with you and with those of them who follow you, all together’). This is the response of Him, the Exalted, to Iblis, and it contains the decree (القضاء) of the Fire against him and those who follow him.

His saying: فَالْحَقُ is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is omitted, or a predicate whose subject is omitted. The particle fa is for the ordering of what follows upon what preceded. "The Truth" (الحق) refers to that which is opposite to falsehood, as supported by the repetition of "the Truth" with the definite article, which certainly refers to the opposite of falsehood. The estimation is: "The Truth is what I swear by: I will surely fill hell..." or "My word is the Truth: I will surely fill..."

His saying: وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ (and the truth I speak) is a parenthetical sentence indicating the inevitability of the decree. It refutes what was alluded to by Iblis’s saying: "I am better than him," etc., regarding Allah’s command to prostrate being other than "right." The advancement of "the Truth" in "and the Truth I speak" and its being defined with the article is to signify restriction (الحصر).

His saying: لَأَمْلَأَنَّ جَهَنَّمَ مِنْكَ وَمِمَّنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ is the text of the decree that He passed. It seems that "from you" (مِنْكَ) refers to the genus of devils (الشياطين) so as to include Iblis, his progeny, and his tribe. His saying: "and with those of them who follow you" refers to the humans from the progeny of Adam.

We have provided extensive discussion on similar verses in Surah al-Hijr and on the story in Surahs al-Baqarah, al-A'raf, and al-Isra; so you should refer to them.

His saying, the Exalted: قُل| ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ (Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, nor am I one of those who fabricate’). This is a return to what was mentioned at the beginning of the Surah and throughout its verses: that the Quran is a reminder and that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is nothing but a warner. It is a refutation of what they cast at him with their saying: "Go on and stand by your gods! This is indeed something intended."

His saying: ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ means any worldly reward of wealth or status. His saying: وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ means I am not among the people of affectation (التكلف)—which is pretension and adorning oneself with what one does not possess.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ (It is just a reminder for all the nations). That is, the Quran is a general reminder for all the worlds (العالمين)—for the groups of people, the various nations, tribes, and others. It is not exclusive to one people over another such that wealth should be taken for its recitation or a reward for its teaching; rather, it is for everyone.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَتَعْلَمُنَّ نَبَأَهُ بَعْدَ حِينٍ (And you will surely come to know its tidings after a while). That is, you will surely know what the Quran has informed of—the promise, the threat, its triumph over other religions, and so on—after a while (بَعْدَ حِينٍ), meaning after the passage of time.

It has been said that "after a while" refers to the Day of Resurrection; it has been said it refers to the day of death; and it has been said it refers to the Day of Badr. It is not far-fetched to say that its "tidings" (نبأه) are diverse and not restricted to any one of these days such that it alone would be intended; rather, it refers to the absolute sense, for each type of its tidings has its own time.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain of narration from Isma'il al-Ju'fi from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): In a hadith mentioning the Ascension (المعراج), from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): قال تعالى : يا محمد. قلت : لبيك يا رب. قال : فيما اختصم الملأ الأعلى؟ قال : قلت : سبحانك لا علم لي إلا ما علمتني. قال : فوضع يده أي يد القدرة بين ثديي ـ فوجدت بردها بين كتفي ـ قال : فلم يسألني عما مضى ولا عما بقي إلا علمته. فقال : يا محمد فيم اختصم الملأ الأعلى؟ قال : قلت : في الكفارات والدرجات والحسنات — "Allah, the Exalted, said: 'O Muhammad.' I said: 'At Your service, O Lord.' He said: 'Regarding what did the Highest Assembly contend?' I said: 'Glory be to You! I have no knowledge except what You have taught me.' He said: 'Then He placed His hand'—meaning the hand of power—'between my breasts, and I felt its coolness between my shoulders.' He said: 'Then He did not ask me about what has passed nor what remains except that I knew it.' He said: 'O Muhammad, regarding what did the Highest Assembly contend?' I said: 'Regarding the expiations (الكفارات), the degrees (الدرجات), and the good deeds (الحسنات).'"

In al-Majma', Ibn Abbas narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: قال لي ربي : أتدري فيم يختصم الملأ الأعلى؟ فقلت : لا. قال : اختصموا في الكفارات والدرجات ـ فأما الكفارات فإسباغ الوضوء في السبرات ـ ونقل الأقدام إلى الجماعات ـ وانتظار الصلاة بعد الصلاة ، وأما الدرجات فإفشاء السلام وإطعام الطعام ـ والصلاة بالليل والناس نيام — "My Lord said to me: 'Do you know regarding what the Highest Assembly contends?' I said: 'No.' He said: 'They contended regarding the expiations and the degrees. As for the expiations, they are: performing ablution thoroughly in the cold, walking to the congregational prayers, and waiting for one prayer after another. As for the degrees, they are: spreading peace (السلام), feeding the hungry, and praying at night while people are asleep.'"

I say: It was also narrated in al-Khisal from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), where he made what he interpreted as expiations an interpretation for degrees and vice versa. It was also narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur—the hadith of al-Majma'—through many chains from several companions from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) with variations in the narrations.

In any case, the context of the verse refuses to align with the content of these narrations, and there is no evidence indicating that the narrations are in the position of interpreting the verse. Perhaps the contention mentioned in them is other than the contention mentioned in the verse.

In Nahj al-Balagha: الحمد لله الذي لبس العز والكبرياء ـ واختارهما لنفسه دون خلقه ، وجعلهما حمى وحرما على غيره ، واصطفاهما لجلاله ، وجعل اللعنة على من نازعه فيهما من عباده ، ثم اختبر بذلك ملائكته المقربين ـ ليميز المتواضعين منهم من المستكبرين ـ فقال سبحانه ـ وهو العالم بمضمرات القلوب ومحجوبات الغيوب : (إِنِّي خالِقٌ بَشَراً مِنْ طِينٍ ـ فَإِذا سَوَّيْتُهُ وَنَفَخْتُ فِيهِ مِنْ رُوحِي فَقَعُوا لَهُ ساجِدِينَ ـ فَسَجَدَ الْمَلائِكَةُ كُلُّهُمْ أَجْمَعُونَ إِلَّا إِبْلِيسَ) ـ اعترضته الحمية فافتخر على آدم بخلقه ـ وتعصب عليه بأصله ـ. فعدو الله إمام المتعصبين وسلف المستكبرين ـ الذي وضع أساس العصبية ، ونازع الله رداء الجبرية ، وأدرع لباس التعزز ، وخلع قناع التذلل ـ ألا ترون كيف صغره الله بتكبره ، ووضعه بترفعه ـ فجعله في الدنيا مدحورا ، وأعد له في الآخرة سعيرا — "Praise be to Allah who clothed Himself in Might and Grandeur, and chose them for Himself to the exclusion of His creation. He made them a sanctuary and a forbidden zone for others, and selected them for His Majesty. He placed a curse upon whoever among His servants disputes with Him regarding them. Then He tested His near angels therewith, to distinguish the humble among them from the arrogant. So He, the Glorified—and He is the Knower of the secrets of hearts and the hidden things of the Unseen—said: 'Indeed I am about to create a human out of clay. So when I have proportioned him and breathed into him of My spirit, then fall down before him in prostration. So the angels prostrated, all of them together, except Iblis.' Zealotry (الحمية) overcame him, so he boasted over Adam because of his creation and was prejudiced against him because of his origin. Thus, the enemy of Allah is the leader of the prejudiced and the predecessor of the arrogant, who laid the foundation of zealotry, disputed with Allah for the robe of Omnipotence, donned the garment of self-exaltation, and cast off the mask of humility. Do you not see how Allah belittled him for his pride and lowered him for his self-elevation? So He made him an outcast in this world and prepared for him a blazing fire in the Hereafter." [From a sermon].

In al-Uyun, with its chain to Muhammad ibn Ubaydah, who said: سألت الرضا عليه‌السلام عن قول الله تعالى لإبليس : «ما مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِما خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَ» قال : يعني بقدرتي وقوتي — "I asked Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Exalted, to Iblis: 'What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My [own] two hands?' He said: 'It means by My power and My strength.'"

I say: A similar narration was reported in al-Tawhid, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

Regarding the story, there are other narrations that we have cited in their respective places in Surahs al-Baqarah, al-A'raf, al-Hijr, and al-Isra; so refer to them.

From Jawami' al-Jami', from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): للمتكلف ثلاث علامات : ينازع من فوقه ، ويتعاطى ما لا ينال ، ويقول ما لا يعلم — "For the fabricator (المتكلف) there are three signs: he disputes with those above him, he reaches for what cannot be attained, and he says what he does not know."

I say: A similar narration was reported in al-Khisal from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) from Luqman in his testament to his son. It was also narrated through Sunni channels, and in some narrations: "he contends with those above him" (ينازل من فوقه).
